data.name: Introduction: Domains of Dread
entries: The Mists reach from the darkest shadows of the multiverse, condemning the wicked and the brave to endless nightmares as prisoners of the Domains of Dread. These grim netherworlds compose the Ravenloft setting and are horrific backdrops for Dungeons & Dragons adventures steeped in fear. In these haunted realms, adventurers might oppose villainous Darklords, seek escape from the Mists, or experience any terrifying adventure you can imagine.

entries.name: Using This Book
entries: This book explores the Domains of Dread and helps you create frightful adventures and heroes ready to confront horrors. It requires the {@book Player's Handbook|XPHB}, {@book Dungeon Master's Guide|XDMG}, and {@book Monster Manual|XMM}.
{@b This introduction} provides an overview of Ravenloft and its mysteries.
{@book Chapter 1|RHW|1} presents subclasses, backgrounds, species, feats, and more for characters preparing to face nightmarish adventures.
{@book Chapter 2|RHW|2} details prominent Domains of Dread, along with their Darklords and adventures within.
{@book Chapter 3|RHW|3} provides tools for Dungeon Masters running horror adventures, including ways to develop frightful campaigns and rules for Haunted Bastions.
{@book Chapter 4|RHW|4} explores how Dungeon Masters can create their own Domains of Dread.
{@book Chapter 5|RHW|5} reveals allies and enemies that might appear in terrifying tales in the Mists or beyond.
{@book The appendix|RHW|6} details the tarokka deck and how to use it in your adventures.

entries.name: Secrets of Ravenloft
entries: Untold mysteries pervade the {@area Domains of Dread|00a|x}, but these truths underpin the setting:

items.name: 1: Ravenloft Is Malleable
items.entry: The domains of Ravenloft are disconnected demiplanes. They don't share a world or a certain reality. Few truths connect them, and even inviolate facts—such as time or death—might change. The only constant is fear.

items.name: 2: The Dark Powers Reign
items.entry: Sinister forces known as the {@area Dark Powers|005|x} manipulate the Domains of Dread. Unknowable and omnipotent, the Dark Powers torment their captives for unfathomable reasons.

items.name: 3: Domains Imprison Darklords
items.entry: Each of Ravenloft's demiplanes imprisons a {@area Darklord|00b|x}, a villain tormented by the Dark Powers. Domains ironically twist Darklords' desires, trapping these villains in cycles of dread and despair.

items.name: 4: The Mists' Clutches
items.entry: Constant fogs known as the {@area Mists|006|x} encircle each domain and might rise anywhere within. The Mists trap individuals within domains and make travel between domains uncertain. They sometimes drag inhabitants of other worlds into the Domains of Dread.

items.name: 5: Heroes Are Few
items.entry: Few oppose the villainy of the Darklords. The domains don't contain benevolent nations or untarnished heroic orders. Those who oppose evil can rely only on themselves, and they might inspire others or attract greater terrors.

items.name: 6: Isolated Islands
items.entry: The Domains of Dread are isolated from one another and the rest of the multiverse. Magic that facilitates travel between planes doesn't function unless the Dark Powers allow it, and most inhabitants know little of realms beyond. Nevertheless, anything from across the multiverse might appear in Ravenloft, albeit with an unsettling twist.

items.name: 7: Rule of Fear
items.entry: The mysteries of the Dark Powers and the Mists facilitate frightful adventures. Whatever inspires terror and aids your horror adventures is the will of the Dark Powers.

entries.name: The Land of the Mists
entries: This section provides you, the DM, an overview of Ravenloft. For details on specific Domains of Dread, see {@book chapter 2|RHW|2}.

entries.name: Nightmare Logic
entries: Ravenloft cultivates dread in defiance of logic. Domains don't abut one another, historians don't record coherent pasts, and communities cloak ignorance in strange traditions. The Domains of Dread operate on the same logic as nightmares: Whatever is the most terrifying is true. The Dark Powers, the Mists, and the nature of domains are purposefully mysterious. Mundane details and the lives of domain inhabitants are constructed on illusions and impossibilities. Should characters realize the surreality of the setting, those revelations only create a stronger sense of existential dread and suggest greater questions. Ultimately, none can ever fully understand the Domains of Dread.

entries.name: The Dark Powers
entries: The Dark Powers are faceless, godlike forces that manipulate reality within the Domains of Dread. Few even suspect their existence. These enigmatic forces help you create horror adventures and serve two complementary roles:

items.name: Narrative
items.entry: The nature and goals of the Dark Powers are mysteries behind the scenes of the setting. Most adventures won't engage with these forces, but that doesn't prevent characters and Darklords from seeking to learn about them, particularly as a means to escape the Domains of Dread.

items.name: Practical
items.entry: The Dark Powers provide a narrative veneer for DM intervention. They are why characters appear in Ravenloft, why the Mists can't be escaped, and why reality-defying strangeness occurs. The desires of the Dark Powers correspond with your needs in creating horror adventures.

entries.name: The Mists
entries: The Mists of Ravenloft appear without warning and move with eerie intention. Areas within the Mists are {@variantrule Heavily Obscured|XPHB}. No amount of wind can disperse the Mists.
The Mists serve the following roles:

items.name: Dark Clutches
items.entry: The Mists serve the Dark Powers. They might steal residents of other worlds or entire locations from elsewhere in the multiverse and trap them in the Domains of Dread. Whether the Mists ever return what they claim depends on the whims of the Dark Powers.

items.name: Domain Borders
items.entry: The Mists surround and isolate every Domain of Dread, preventing easy or predictable travel between them.

items.name: Transportation
items.entry: The Mists can appear anywhere, claim individuals, and spirit them elsewhere in the Domains of Dread.

entries.title: Few claimed by the Mists escape the horrors within

entries.name: Mysteries of the Mists
entries: The inhabitants of the Domains of Dread ascribe sinister stories to the Mists but accept the Mists as a natural part of their homeland. Those who travel between domains refer to their world as the Land of the Mists. Any supernatural happening or malicious force might be blamed on the Mists. Those who interact with the Mists—or who come from beyond—are typically met with suspicion.

entries.name: Mist Talismans
entries: Mist talismans are nonmagical objects that resonate with certain domains. They might be any object, and some might be aligned with multiple domains. By holding or wearing a Mist talisman and focusing on the domain it's associated with, a creature in the Mists can reach the intended domain after {@dice 1d6} hours of travel. Creatures following within 30 feet of a Mist talisman's bearer reach the same destination. Mist talismans associated with domains are detailed in {@book chapter 2|RHW|2}.

Mist talismans don't allow their bearers to escape domains with closed borders (see "{@area Darklords|00b|x}"). Similarly, if the borders of the destination domain are closed, roll on the Wandering the Mists table to determine what happens.

entries.name: Wandering the Mists
entries: So long as a Darklord hasn't closed their domain's borders, creatures that enter the Mists might travel to other domains. When characters without a Mist talisman enter the Mists surrounding a domain, they emerge elsewhere after {@dice 1d6} hours of travel. Roll on or take inspiration from the Wandering the Mists table to determine where the Mists relocate characters.

entries.caption: Wandering the Mists
rows: In a domain of your choosing.
After wandering for another {@dice 1d6} hours and rolling again on this table.
A mile from where they entered at midnight of the following night.
Inside a structure within the domain they tried to leave, perhaps a cave, crypt, shed, or closet.

After encountering creatures within the Mists. Roll any die. If you roll an even number, a terrified {@creature Commoner|XMM} appears. If you roll an odd number, a {@creature Mist Horror|RHW} (see {@area chapter 5|3ab|x}) appears. Roll again on this table.
Where they entered, only to find the domain changed. Perhaps someone the characters knew is gone and no one remembers the person.
On a world on the Material Plane. After {@dice 1d6} hours, the Mists rise around them once more and return them to the domain they departed.

entries.name: Domains
entries: The Domains of Dread are untold numbers of individual demiplanes hidden in the Shadowfell. Some are created entirely by the {@area Dark Powers|005|x}, while others the Mists claimed from elsewhere in the multiverse and bear evidence of other worlds. Domains might change as the Dark Powers find new ways to torment their captives, with domains changing shape, merging, fading away, reappearing, and even repeating events.
The Domains of Dread share these features:

items.name: Domains Are Finite
items.entry: Domains vary in size from countries or regions to a single city or structure.

items.name: Domains Are Isolated
items.entry: Each domain is bordered by the {@area Mists|006|x}. Generally, domains don't share geography, and the residents and cultures of different domains don't influence one another.

items.name: Domains Hold Darklords
items.entry: Each domain exists to contains a Darklord. Darklords exert some control over their domains, but they are tormented by their imprisonment. A domain without a Darklord has no purpose; if a domain's Darklord escapes or is permanently defeated, it might cease to exist.

entries: {@book Chapter 2|RHW|2} describes some of the most notorious Domains of Dread, while {@book chapter 3|RHW|3} explores how to use domains in horror adventures. Use the methods in {@book chapter 4|RHW|4} to create domains of your own.

entries.name: Darklords
entries: Every domain torments a Darklord, a villain whose depravity attracted the attention of the {@area Dark Powers|005|x} and led to their imprisonment in the {@area Domains of Dread|00a|x}. A Darklord might be anyone in a domain, from a national leader to an unassuming resident. Darklords share these qualities:

items.name: Darklords Are Evil
items.entry: Each Darklord is the ultimate villain of a domain and the root of its terrors.

items.name: Darklords Are Prisoners
items.entry: Darklords can't leave their domains. If a Darklord enters the {@area Mists|006|x} or uses another method to attempt escape, the Dark Powers return them to their domain. Even death rarely allows a Darklord to escape for long.

items.name: Darklords Are Tormented
items.entry: Every domain torments its Darklord in an ironic way tied to their crimes, failures, or ambitions.

items.name: Darklords Influence Domains
items.entry: Darklords have supernatural influence over their domains. A Darklord can close their domain's borders (see "Closing Domain Borders" below) and create other effects.

items.name: Darklords Are Active
items.entry: Darklords might brood over their failures, but their attempts to achieve their depraved goals fuel their conflicts with characters. Liberally use Darklords in your adventures rather than saving them for climactic plots.

items.name: Darklords Don't Realize They're Darklords
items.entry: Darklords generally don't realize they occupy a special place in the world. Their obsessions make them oblivious to the strangeness of their circumstances. Most don't suspect the nature of the Domains of Dread or the Dark Powers.

items.name: Closing Domain Borders
items.entry: Darklords can close the borders of their domains at will. When this occurs, the Mists become dangerous (see the Darklord descriptions in {@book chapter 2|RHW|2}), and no matter how far a creature travels in the Mists, it eventually finds itself back in the domain it left. This makes leaving a domain impossible until the Darklord opens the borders or is defeated.

entries: Chapter 2 presents stat blocks for numerous Darklords. {@book Chapter 3|RHW|3} explores how to use Darklords in your horror adventures, while {@book chapter 4|RHW|4} aids in creating your own Darklords.

entries.name: People of the Mists
entries: The inhabitants of the {@area Domains of Dread|00a|x} fall into these categories:

items.name: Soulless Shells
items.entry: Some domain inhabitants are creations of the {@area Dark Powers|005|x}. These beings are indistinguishable from normal creatures, living unremarkable lives and existing to reinforce whatever status quo the Dark Powers impose. At the will of the Dark Powers, these inhabitants might reappear in a domain even after being slain.

items.name: Bright Souls
items.entry: Some beings are like those who populate other worlds in the multiverse. Some live unremarkable lives, while others awake to their homes' wrongness and fight their domains' evils.

items.name: Strangers
items.entry: Those whom the {@area Mists|006|x} abduct from other worlds likely suspect the surreal nature of the Domains of Dread. These individuals often seek ways to escape the Mists and return home.

entries.name: Death and Rebirth
entries: The spirits of those who die in the Domains of Dread or the Mists don't travel to the afterlife. Those magically returned to life after being dead for at least 24 hours return with the grim realization that they're trapped within the Mists.

The souls of those who die and aren't returned to life are trapped within the Domains of Dread. The Dark Powers might eventually reincarnate these souls. Individuals who inherit a soul often look like their predecessor and might recall memories of their past lives.

entries.name: Magic in the Mists
entries: Some magic functions differently in the {@area Domains of Dread|00a|x}. Characters who receive spells from otherworldly forces or patrons do so as normal, but the magic noted here functions in distinct ways:

items.name: Banishment
items.entry: Aberrations, Celestials, Elementals, Fey, or Fiends aren't transported to different planes after the {@spell Banishment|XPHB} spell ends. Rather, they appear in a random different domain.

items.name: Escape
items.entry: No spell allows escape from Ravenloft.

items.name: Etherealness
items.entry: Magic that accesses the Border Ethereal, such as the {@spell Etherealness|XPHB} spell, functions as normal, but a creature that enters the Border Ethereal from a domain is pulled back into the domain it left on leaving the Ethereal Plane.

items.name: Extradimensional Spaces
items.entry: Extradimensional spaces function as normal, but spells cast within them are subject to the same restrictions as magic cast within a domain.

items.name: Otherworldly Contact
items.entry: Magic that allows contact with beings from other planes, such as the {@spell Commune|XPHB} spell, functions normally, but the domain's Darklord senses when someone in their domain casts such a spell. The Darklord can make themself the spell's target so that they become the one who is contacted and answer as they please.

items.name: Planar Boundaries
items.entry: For the purposes of spell effects that change across or are blocked by planar boundaries (such as the {@spell Sending|XPHB} spell), each domain is considered its own plane.

items.name: Planar Travel
items.entry: Magic that allows travel to other planes, such as the {@spell Astral Projection|XPHB} and {@spell Plane Shift|XPHB} spells, fails in the Domains of Dread. {@spell Gate|XPHB} can conjure beings into the Domains of Dread, but it doesn't allow them or others to escape. Magic that creates or sends creatures to demiplanes—such as the {@spell Demiplane|XPHB}, {@spell Maze|XPHB}, and {@spell Imprisonment|XPHB} spells—functions as normal.

items.name: Summoning
items.entry: Magic that summons creatures or objects functions as normal, but whatever is summoned is a creation of the {@area Dark Powers|005|x} and might appear or behave in eerie ways.

entries.name: Cosmetic Magic Effects
entries: Magic effects produced in Ravenloft appear more sinister than they do elsewhere in the multiverse. For example, a creature conjured by {@spell Find Familiar|XPHB} or {@spell Find Steed|XPHB} might appear unnatural or deathly, or the {@spell Find the Path|XPHB} spell might cause a silent, childlike spirit to guide the caster to their destination. Describe such effects in whatever way reinforces a horrific atmosphere, drawing inspiration from the domain where your adventure takes place.

entries.name: Life in the Mists
entries: The {@area Domains of Dread|00a|x} might seem surreal to individuals from other worlds, but for the residents of the {@area Mists|006|x}, Ravenloft's bizarre qualities are normal parts of reality.

entries.name: Shared Delusion
entries: Domain residents don't see incongruities in their existence. Societal pressure and soulless individuals controlled by the {@area Dark Powers|005|x} encourage inhabitants to embrace ignorance, dismiss coincidences, or doubt their senses. Residents of the Domains of Dread are no less perceptive than people elsewhere in the multiverse, but their home domains manipulate them into believing everything is as it should be, must be, and always was.

Those who call domain residents' reality into question are likely to be dismissed, distrusted, or worse. Individuals who speak of other worlds are broadly considered to be liars or delusional. Few individuals are open-minded to such concepts.

entries.name: Adventurers
entries: Ravenloft rarely gives rise to heroes. For the inhabitants of most domains, survival is the only act of courage they pursue. Those willing to confront the terrors of the {@area Mists|006|x} are typically strangers from other worlds or domain residents jarred from their apathy by traumatic events.

Adventurers find few peers in the Land of the Mists. Guilds of adventurers and shops catering to their needs generally don't exist. As a result, the challenge of confronting evil rests squarely with a few rare souls. {@book Chapter 3|RHW|3} provides details on wanderers who might aid adventurers across domains.

entries.name: Culture and Technology
entries: Each domain has a distinct culture. Such cultures might be inspired by societies elsewhere in the multiverse or fantasies manufactured to torment a {@area Darklord|00b|x}. Traditions are purportedly based on history, but few domains have coherent records of the past. Details of ages before a Darklord's arrival are usually vague or absent.

Similarly, every domain has its own level of technological advancement, the details of which are up to you. In some domains, firearms and clockwork devices are common, while others might feature cutting-edge weird science.
No cultural exchange occurs between the {@area Domains of Dread|00a|x}. While evidence of one domain might appear in another, such things are viewed with suspicion.

entries.name: Currency
entries: Some domains mint their own gold, silver, and copper coins. Though these currencies bear different markings, merchants aren't particular about the designs stamped on their currency. Platinum and electrum coins rarely circulate through the domains, but they appear often enough—originating from hidden troves or ancient vaults—that no trader questions their value.

entries.name: Language
entries: Common is the shared language of the Domains of Dread. Some domains might have unique words, and speakers from different domains might have distinctive accents, but individuals from different domains are understandable to one another. Other {@table Choose Languages; Standard Languages|XPHB|languages} from the {@book Player's Handbook|XPHB} are also known among the domains, but their rarity varies between domains as you see fit.

entries.name: Religion
entries: In many domains, residents worship stern but vague gods, and clergy have scant supernatural powers. Any deity, patron, or philosophy from anywhere in the multiverse might have followers in the {@area Domains of Dread|00a|x}, though these followers' beliefs might be unconventional. The {@area Dark Powers|005|x} also breathe power into the faith of delusional zealots. Whether otherworldly forces or the Dark Powers empower faithful spellcasters is up to you.

entries.name: Ezra
entries: Mysteriously, worship of one god has its origins in the {@area Mists|006|x}. The god Ezra is depicted as a vaporous figure with dark, billowing hair and is believed to be able to manipulate the Mists. Ezra's holy symbol is a sprig of belladonna atop a silver kite shield. Ezra's followers across domains view the deity in contradictory ways. For some, Ezra is a stalwart guardian, while others perceive the god as a soul-stealing embodiment of the Mists. The truths of Ezra's existence and nature are mysteries.

entries.name: Time and Dates
entries: The Domains of Dread don't share a calendar. However, in most domains, locals measure time by "moons" rather than months. Each moon begins on the first night of a full moon and lasts a full lunar cycle. A year consists of twelve moons.

Although domains don't subscribe to a shared history, the populace of all domains inexplicably accept the current year as 735. Some local histories cleave to anomalous dates and methods of tracking years, but these are considered obsolete. No such irregularities exist in {@book Barovia|RHW|2}, though, where year 1 corresponds with the founding of Barovia by the von Zarovich family.

entries.name: Travel
entries: Most domain residents have little interest in domains beyond their own, and the nature of the Mists makes mercantile ventures and military conquests between domains essentially impossible. Nevertheless, rare individuals travel between the domains either at random, using {@area Mist talismans|008|x} (see "{@area The Mists|006|x}"), or by engaging mist-wandering guides. Those who enter the Mists by road, river, or sea usually emerge in a new domain by the same method. This means that domains with no coastline must be reached by methods other than ship, but also that travelers following roads rarely emerge from the Mists on the sea—though they might arrive inland within a domain surrounded by water.

entries.name: Pronunciation Guide
entries: The Ravenloft Pronunciations table lists how to pronounce the names of prominent Domains of Dread, Darklords, and other residents of Ravenloft.

data.name: Chapter 1: Character Options

entries.title: The Weathermay-Foxgrove Twins, Gennifer and Laurie, along with Savra Sunstar and Ez d'Avenir, explore the infamous House on Gryphon Hill

entries: Whether for a night or an eternity, the Mists of Ravenloft pit heroes against their greatest nightmares. This chapter presents resources for players creating and playing characters in horror adventures, including new {@area subclasses|024|x}, {@area backgrounds|084|x}, {@area species|089|x}, and {@area feats|0a1|x}. These character options supplement the rules presented in the {@book Player's Handbook|XPHB}.

entries.name: Haunted Heroes
entries: This section highlights ways you can create a character prepared to participate in horror adventures.

entries.name: Before You Begin
entries: Who is a horror adventure supposed to be scary for: you as the player or your character? Is it suspenseful like a mystery story or frightening like a slasher movie? What do you not want to see in a horror adventure? Align with your group's DM and other players on topics like these before participating in fear-focused adventures. Horror tales cover broad swaths of content. What some might find unsettling, others might shrug off. If you are the DM, consult the "{@book Ensuring Fun for All|XDMG|0|Ensuring Fun for All}" section of the {@book Dungeon Master's Guide|XDMG} for guidance on setting expectations about your game.

entries.name: Building a Horror Hero

entries.title: Characters in horror adventures are often haunted by greater terrors than the monsters they face

entries: While playing a horror adventure, you participate in building a sense of dread. If your character laughs in the face of every danger, they undermine the adventure's atmosphere. Consider how past events might haunt your character and contribute to the mood of horror adventures.

entries.name: Prepare to Be Scared
entries: Knowing what frightens your character provides insight into their past and can motivate their behavior. A character's fear of cats might stem from a terrible sight they witnessed at their grandmother's home, while a fear of earthquakes might tie back to the experience of being trapped after a tremor. Consider two or three things that unsettle your character and how those fears shape your character's past and present.

Courage isn't the absence of fear, but how one deals with and overcomes it. Consider this in the context of your character. How might they react to a startling scene before rallying to overcome it? Does your character scream and flee, freeze in place, or jump recklessly into battle? Record a default fear reaction on your character sheet to help you respond when shocking events occur.

entries.name: Fear Stays in the Game
entries: Understand the difference between scaring characters and scaring players. For example, if you know another player has a fear of spiders, never employ that knowledge when contributing to a creepy scene. The {@book Dungeon Master's Guide|XDMG} offers guidance (see "{@book Hard and Soft Limits|XDMG|0|Hard and Soft Limits}") to ensure your group has a method for raising concerns mid-game, in the event that things become too intense or go in directions you don't want to explore. If something in the game leads you to consider scaring or otherwise involving another player's character, ask that player's permission before acting. Their enjoyment is more important than shock value.

entries.name: Sinister Secrets
entries: Many characters in horror adventures have shadowy pasts: a harrowing experience that haunts their dreams, a bargain with sinister forces they keep hidden from others, or some other nefarious secret. How a character engages with such internal darkness can make for exciting conflicts. Be sure that your choices allow your character to remain a reliable part of your adventuring group, though, and not a near-villain other heroes only tolerate.

If you'd like your character's secret to have mechanical implications, Dark Gifts (detailed later in {@area this chapter|0a7|x}) grant characters supernatural abilities in return for sinister consequences.

entries.name: Enjoying the Struggle
entries: Not everyone can escape a horror story unscathed. While your character should do everything they can to triumph over challenges, any scars they gain along the way are part of what makes the horror meaningful and memorable.

Consider your goals as a participant in a horror adventure. Are you aiming to ensure the party's survival or just your character's? Or do you simply want to create a memorable experience for the other players? Let your goals evolve during an adventure. If these goals lead to greater tension, drama, or sacrifice in ways that echo your favorite horror tales—and that don't undermine other players' enjoyment of the game—embrace them. Your character's actions have great influence in creating exciting and impactful horror adventures.

entries.name: Subclasses
entries: This section presents seven new subclasses: the {@class Artificer|EFA|Reanimator (Artificer)|Reanimator|RHW}, {@class Bard|XPHB|College of Spirits (Bard)|Spirits|RHW}, {@class Cleric|XPHB|Grave Domain (Cleric)|Grave|RHW}, {@class Ranger|XPHB|Hollow Warden (Ranger)|Hollow Warden|RHW}, {@class Rogue|XPHB|Phantom (Rogue)|Phantom|RHW}, {@class Sorcerer|XPHB|Shadow Sorcery (Sorcerer)|Shadow|RHW}, and {@class Warlock|XPHB|Undead Patron (Warlock)|Undead|RHW}. The {@class Artificer|EFA} class is detailed in {@book Eberron: Forge of the Artificer|EFA}.

items: {@class Artificer|EFA|Reanimator (Artificer)|Reanimator|RHW}
{@class Bard|XPHB|College of Spirits (Bard)|Spirits|RHW}
{@class Cleric|XPHB|Grave Domain (Cleric)|Grave|RHW}
{@class Ranger|XPHB|Hollow Warden (Ranger)|Hollow Warden|RHW}
{@class Rogue|XPHB|Phantom (Rogue)|Phantom|RHW}
{@class Sorcerer|XPHB|Shadow Sorcery (Sorcerer)|Shadow|RHW}
{@class Warlock|XPHB|Undead Patron (Warlock)|Undead|RHW}

entries.name: Backgrounds
entries: This section presents backgrounds exploring origins suited to characters in horror adventures, using the same format presented in the {@book Player's Handbook|XPHB}.

Two of the backgrounds, {@background Mist Wanderer|RHW} and {@background Spirit Medium|RHW}, offer {@area Dark Gift feats|0a7|x} rather than Origin feats; these feats appear at the end of the chapter. The {@background Haunted One|RHW} and {@background Investigator|RHW} backgrounds provide a choice between an Origin feat and a Dark Gift feat, so you can tailor your character's relationship to sinister forces in their past.

items: {@background Haunted One|RHW}
{@background Investigator|RHW}
{@background Mist Wanderer|RHW}
{@background Spirit Medium|RHW}

entries.name: Species
entries: Remarkable events and supernatural horrors have transformed individuals, creating species that spread throughout the Mists and the multiverse. A character might have been born as a member of one of the species in this section or transformed into one after harrowing events.

items: {@race Dhampir|RHW}
{@race Hexblood|RHW}
{@race Lupin|RHW}
{@race Reborn|RHW}

entries.name: Feats
entries: This section presents new feats tailored for characters adventuring through Ravenloft. Included are a handful of new feats in the Origin category, as well as a new type of feat: {@area Dark Gift feats|0a7|x}, which represent sinister bargains a character might make with mysterious forces.

entries.name: Origin Feats
entries: These feats are in the Origin category.

items: {@feat Sharp Eye|RHW}
{@feat Survivor|RHW}

entries.name: Dark Gifts
entries: The Dark Powers influence many who struggle within their clutches, tempting both the innocent and the ambitious with whispered promises. These sinister bargains come in dreams or mysterious visions. Their terms are always clear and their prices terrible.

With the DM's permission, whenever you would gain a feat from the Origin category, you can instead gain a Dark Gift feat. A DM can bestow a Dark Gift feat on a character during the course of an adventure, representing an insidious deal with a Darklord or some other malevolent force.

items: {@feat Aberrant Anatomy|RHW}
{@feat Echoing Soul|RHW}
{@feat Gathered Whispers|RHW}
{@feat Living Shadow|RHW}
{@feat Mist Walker|RHW}
{@feat Second Skin|RHW}
{@feat Symbiotic Being|RHW}
{@feat Touch of Death|RHW}
{@feat Watchers|RHW}

data.name: Chapter 2: Domains of Ravenloft

entries.title: By the light of the full moon, villagers in Kartakass undergo a terrifying transformation

entries: At the unfathomable edge of the Shadowfell liethe Domains of Dread, realms bound by Mists where magic and reality twist into nightmare. This chapter for DMs explores the most terrifying of the Domains of Dread. Among other tools, each featured domain includes the following details:

items.name: Domain
items.entry: This initial section introduces the domain, its unique horrors, and notable locations.

items.name: Darklord
items.entry: The domain's Darklord, their stat block, effects they create within their domain, how they close their domain's borders (see the {@area introduction|00b|x}), and details of their lair appear here.

items.name: Adventures
items.entry: This section provides guidance on creating adventures in the domain, a campaign outline, and short adventures.

entries: This chapter concludes with an overview of {@book additional domains|RHW|2}.

entries.name: Tarokka and the Mists
entries: Tarokka fortune-telling cards (detailed in the {@book appendix|RHW|6}) have special relationships with domains and their Darklords.

entries.name: Tarokka Mist Talismans
entries: Each Domain of Dread is aligned with a tarokka card. A character with a tarokka deck can use it to travel between domains. While in the Mists, the deck's bearer can draw a random card from the deck; that card serves as a Mist talisman (see the {@area introduction|008|x}), allowing travel to the domain it is aligned with. A tarokka deck can be used in this way once every 24 hours. A list of domains and their aligned tarokka cards appears later in {@book this book|RHW|2|Random Domains of Dread}.

entries.name: Darklords and the Tarokka
entries: Every Domain of Dread has a tarokka card that opposes it—one hinting at the truth of the domain's Darklord. If a domain's opposed tarokka card is drawn during a tarokka reading in that domain, that domain's Darklord becomes aware of the reading and its participants. When this occurs, the Darklord can choose to see through the eyes of the tarokka user and speak through them for 1 minute. The Darklord might make their presence known, level a threat, or misinterpret the cards. A tarokka reader has no memory of what a Darklord uses them to say.

entries.name: Domains of Ravenloft Guide
entries: The table below lists the Dread Domains and the respective Darklord that resides there.

entries.name: Barovia
entries: {@i Domain of the First Vampire}

items.name: Darklord:
items.entry: {@area Strahd von Zarovich|0d5|x}

items.name: Genre:
items.entry: {@area Gothic horror|37b|x}

items.name: Hallmarks:
items.entry: Undead despot, notorious haunted stronghold, tragic resurrection

items.name: Mist Talismans:
items.entry: Barovian wine bottle, von Zarovich crest, Mark of the Raven talisman

items.name: Aligned Tarokka Card:
items.entry: Darklord

items.name: Opposed Tarokka Card:
items.entry: Mists

entries: In Barovia, the night is a curse. As darkness falls, wicked souls slip from the spires of Castle Ravenloft to work the will of an immortal overlord. This is the realm of the vampire {@creature Strahd von Zarovich|RHW|Count Strahd von Zarovich}, whose depravities have doomed him and his land to endless cycles of obsession and despair.

The howls of wolves and shrieks of ravens echo through the dismal valleys and oppressive forests of Barovia. In isolated communities, superstitious villagers find the brightness in their lives smothered by dread of their aloof overlord and other festering evils. These Barovians fear the night, as agents of the devil Strahd watch in the dark and claim whatever he desires. Yet few in Barovia realize their torments have played out again and again. In every generation, echoes of Strahd's past reemerge, compelling and confounding the Darklord as he endlessly obsesses over the one victim who's eluded him for centuries.

entries.name: Domain Features
entries: Barovia is known for the following features:

items.name: The Devil Strahd
items.entry: Few have seen {@area Count von Zarovich|0d5|x}, but all know he dwells in Castle Ravenloft, a citadel from which few visitors return. Strahd is infamous for viciously executing any who flagrantly break his laws, but otherwise he rarely interferes in the lives of his people. Some whisper that he is a vampire.

items.name: Haunted Tales
items.entry: The stories of Barovia's people are full of hidden evils: accursed ruins, portentous ravens, secretive cults, treacherous witches, vicious werewolves, and worse.

items.name: Isolation and Suspicion
items.entry: Barovia is a realm of wolf-prowled forests and treacherous mountains where the sun is obscured. The land's superstitious people live in scattered villages. They distrust strangers, fearing they're agents of the count.

entries.name: Locations in Barovia
entries: Barovia is a land of long shadows where ancient wildernesses strangle the ruins of a near-forgotten empire. Mysteries pervade the domain, and those who leave the relative safety of Barovia's dismal settlements enter perilous lands where mortals aren't welcome.
The village of Barovia is described below, and other notable locations are summarized on the {@book Barovia Locations table|RHW|2|Barovia Locations}.

entries.name: Village of Barovia
entries: Lying in the shadow of Castle Ravenloft, the village of Barovia teeters on the brink of ruin. Its few residents rarely venture from their homes, suspecting their neighbors of wickedness and fearing evils in the night. Their dread is largely justified, as wolves, ghosts, and vampire spawn haunt the town, and many locals have been consumed by callousness or wicked temptations.

A rare exception to the village's cold desperation can be found at the local tavern, the Blood of the Vine. Those few who oppose Strahd's evil congregate here—under the covert oversight of the count's agents.

entries.caption: Barovia Locations
rows: A society of mages constructed the Amber Temple long ago to imprison vestiges of malevolent entities. The mages are long dead; mighty evil forces now obsess over the temple's secrets.
The fortified Argynvostholt mansion lies in ruins haunted by the restless spirits of a knightly order destroyed by Strahd. A statue of a regal silver dragon stands before the manor's entrance.
Barovia spans valleys among the Balinok Mountains. Snow crowns the highest peak, Mount Baratok, while the smaller Mount Ghakis is riddled with deep caves.
The grim castle of Count Strahd von Zarovich looms over the village of Barovia. The haunted castle contains Strahd's supernatural servants and the secrets of the Darklord's origins.
Mighty gates flanked by decapitated statues of soldiers stand along the Old Svalich Road. They open mysteriously for travelers but allow only Vistani to leave the domain to the east.
Above the fortified, wooded village of Krezk rises the Abbey of Saint Markovia. Here, a mysterious abbot tends to patients but, in doing so, leaves them permanently changed.
An ominous windmill called Old Bonegrinder is home to a coven of night hags that create magical pastries. Those who consume the pastries enter a stupor filled with vivid, joyful dreams.
Moldering ruins are all that remain of Berez, a village that denied Strahd's will and earned his wrath. Nearby stands a statue of a kneeling peasant clutching a rose.
Packs of wolves, dire wolves, and werewolves prowl the Svalich Woods, hunting those who oppose Strahd. Strange hermits and witches lurk within the deepest reaches.

The Vistani who camp near the Tser Pool lake are often joined by other wanderers. This is also the home of the seer {@creature Madam Eva|RHW} (see {@area chapter 5|3a9|x}), who knows much of Strahd and the Mists.
The walled town of Vallaki hosts regular festivals to lift the spirits of its despondent inhabitants. Those who don't participate risk being punished as malcontents.
A cult of druids devoted to Strahd conducts rituals amid the circle of boulders atop Yester Hill. The Mists nearby reflect a vision of the count's ancestral home in the distance.

entries.name: Strahd von Zarovich
entries: {@i First of the Darklords}
I am the Ancient. I am the Land. My beginnings are lost in the darkness of the past. I am not dead. Nor am I alive. I am undead, forever.

Count Strahd von Zarovich has lost all he ever desired—over and over again. Tormented by the Dark Powers, Strahd is haunted by visions of his lost family and tarnished nobility. The count spends long years brooding in the depths of Castle Ravenloft, but a reincarnation of his obsession appears every generation: Tatyana. Each time Tatyana's reincarnation emerges, Strahd seeks to undo the failures of his past and find a way to win her love. Time and time again, Strahd's heartless, domineering nature causes his bloody history to repeat itself and drives Tatyana beyond his clutches.

A severe, aristocratic figure, the count has the bearing of a former soldier and the impatience of one used to being obeyed. He feels little but occasionally embraces the novelty of toying with amusing strangers or breaking the spirits of his enemies. During his unnaturally long existence, he has learned many occult secrets and mysteries of the Domains of Dread. Strahd's dignified bearing crumbles when someone breaks his laws or denies him what he desires. In such cases, Strahd makes bloody examples of his opponents, drawing out his vengeance into campaigns of terror.

entries.name: Strahd's Tale
entries: Count Strahd von Zarovich was a ruthless conqueror. Retiring after years of war, Strahd settled in the beautiful valley where he'd won his greatest victory. There, in the way of his ancestors, he spilled his blood into the earth, sealing a pact between himself and the land. In honor of his parents, King Barov and Queen Ravenovia, he named the valley Barovia and constructed his home, Castle Ravenloft.

Strahd invited his few remaining family members to live with him at Castle Ravenloft and eventually was joined by his younger brother, Sergei. Sergei was everything Strahd wasn't—youthful, empathic, and warm. Soon after he arrived, the younger von Zarovich and a local villager named Tatyana fell in love. Strahd resented his brother, even more so after meeting Tatyana and being charmed by her pure kindness. Obsessed, Strahd sought to woo her but was rebuffed. Unwilling to accept her wishes or the idea of Sergei as his better, Strahd made a pact with ancient evil forces to regain his vitality and ensnare Tatyana's heart.

The day of Sergei and Tatyana's wedding, Strahd murdered Sergei and, to seal his pact, drank his brother's blood. He revealed his new might to Tatyana, expecting to enthrall her. Instead, horrified by Sergei's murder, Tatyana fled Strahd, ultimately leaping from the height of Castle Ravenloft and vanishing into the Mists. At the same time, traitors from among the castle's guards and wedding's guests rose up to assassinate Strahd. Despite countless wounds, Strahd didn't die. His bargain with the Dark Powers had made Strahd the multiverse's first vampire. A night of slaughter followed; when dawn touched the parapets of Castle Ravenloft, no living soul survived within. But Strahd remained.

Strahd's villainy and his connection to his land drew all of Barovia into the grip of the Dark Powers, which transplanted the vampire and his realm into the Shadowfell. This began a succession of torments that plague the count still. The Dark Powers' attentions have spilled forth to drag others into the Mists as well, giving rise to the Domains of Dread.

entries.name: Strahd's Domain
entries: Barovia is altered by Strahd's presence, creating the following effects:

items.name: Closing the Borders
items.entry: While in his domain, Strahd can close or open the borders to Barovia at will. While the borders are closed, noxious fog suffuses the Mists surrounding Barovia. A creature that enters the Mists for the first time on a turn or starts its turn there must succeed on a {@dc 20} Constitution saving throw or gain 1 {@condition Exhaustion|XPHB} level. A creature makes this save only once per turn.

items.name: Dreadful Darkness
items.entry: While in an area of {@variantrule Darkness|XPHB}, creatures other than Strahd and creatures of his choice have {@variantrule Disadvantage|XPHB} on saving throws to end or avoid the {@condition Frightened|XPHB} condition.

items.name: Jealous Heart
items.entry: When a character receives {@variantrule Heroic Inspiration|XPHB} but already has it, that character can't give it to another character in the group.

entries: If Strahd dies, these effects end immediately.

entries.name: Strahd's Lairs
entries: While the notorious Castle Ravenloft is Strahd's home, it isn't his only redoubt in Barovia. Multiple bastions of evil serve as sanctuaries for the count and his servants, should fate demand.

entries.title: Nightly the village of Vallaki bars its gates against attacks from wolves and deadlier predators

entries.name: Castle Ravenloft
entries: Castle Ravenloft is Strahd von Zarovich's accursed sanctuary. Tormented spirits and Strahd's minions haunt the vast castle, each serving the count's schemes, reflecting some aspect of his depraved past, or protecting his coffin in the castle catacombs. Mementos of lost times and fallen heroes lie throughout the count's home, some of which might hold the key to his undoing. The adventure {@adventure Curse of Strahd|CoS} details Castle Ravenloft.

entries.name: Von Zarovich Sanctuaries
entries: Hidden across Barovia lie secret sanctuaries known only to Strahd and his servants. These crypt-like boltholes are hidden from the sun and exist primarily to shelter the count should he require. Over the centuries, Strahd has granted trusted servants access to some of these sanctuaries and used others in plots or experiments that he keeps distant from Castle Ravenloft. Still others Strahd has forgotten, allowing other evils to fester there. An example of one of these sites is depicted on {@book map 2.2|RHW|2|Map 2.2: Blood of the Vine Tavern} and has the following features:

items.name: Count's Coffin
items.entry: Hidden in each sanctuary lies a coffin bearing the von Zarovich crest. This might be obvious or in a space only accessible to vampires.

items.name: The Lord's Presence
items.entry: Behind a vault-like iron door, an entry chamber holds a high-backed throne. Strahd forbids servants from using these symbols of his authority.

items.name: Servants' Space
items.entry: Alcoves carved in the walls provide space for the coffins or bunks of Strahd's living or dead servants. Sometimes, the Darklord uses these spaces for arcane experiments or as makeshift prisons.

entries.name: Adventures in Barovia
entries: Adventures set in Barovia involve oppressive, gothic menace; suspicion; and the legendary threat of Strahd von Zarovich. Use this section to develop horror adventures in this domain.

entries.title: Castle Ravenloft, the accursed home of Count Strahd von Zarovich, casts its shadows across the village of Barovia and far beyond

entries.name: Horror in Barovia
entries: Adventures in Barovia often include the following elements.

entries.name: The Ancient, The Land
entries: Strahd remains bound to Barovia and it to him in a way few rulers or Darklords understand. He knows when any creature enters or dies violently within Barovia, and he takes offense when someone slays his servants or the domain's wolves. Strahd can manifest a variety of dramatic effects, such as casting his voice on the wind, making his visage appear in the clouds, or changing the weather. These effects don't aid him in combat, but they contribute to making him feel like an oppressive and ever-present threat.

entries.name: Incarnations of the Past
entries: The subject of Strahd's obsession, Tatyana, perpetually reincarnates among the people of Barovia. No matter what form she takes, the Darklord unceasingly seeks her, determined to possess her and make her part of an idealized fantasy of his past. Tatyana's incarnation might be anyone, including a player character. If her incarnation is an NPC, roll on the Tatyana's Reincarnation and Connection to Strahd tables to inspire a new chapter in the count's tale of obsession.

entries.caption: Tatyana's Reincarnation
rows: Anna, the keeper of what another Darklord desires—such as {@area Ankhtepot's ka|178|x} or {@area Mordenheim's Unbreakable Heart|202|x} (both detailed later in this book).
{@creature Ez d'Avenir|RHW} (see {@area chapter 5|3a3|x}), a monster hunter.
Ireena Kolyana, adopted daughter of Burgomaster Indirovich of the village of Barovia.
Lyssa von Zarovich, a distant relative of Strahd's, who has become a vampire.
Nasseri and Yasmine, identical twins who serve the god Ezra.
Petra Rilenovich, an artist who paints images of what she calls "dream lives."
Quentin L'Argent, the dragonborn son of herders living near Argynvostholt.
Renoir Laurent, the son of Chantal Laurent, a woman Strahd abducted years ago.
Vanasia, a member of a far-ranging Vistani band or other group of mist wanderers.
Vasilka, a {@creature Flesh Golem|XMM} or {@race reborn|RHW} who lives in the abbey of Krezk.

entries.caption: Connection to Strahd
rows: Has weaponized their blood with poison that will put Strahd to sleep for a generation.
Is hidden from Strahd by another Darklord or some more mysterious evil.
Becomes trapped by a deceitful servant of Strahd, either bodily or by an effect such as a {@spell Magic Jar|XPHB} spell.
Knows of their reincarnations and seeks to end them by freeing Barovia from the Mists.
Lives a charmed life, unknowingly protected and cultivated by the count.
Misguidedly plans to redeem Strahd through their exceptional kindness or faith.
Uses magic to hide from Strahd by appearing elderly, childlike, or as a mysterious animal.
Searches for a relic that can weaken Strahd.
Seeks to join a group of adventurers and organize a siege of Castle Ravenloft.
Wants to resurrect past incarnations of Tatyana to battle Strahd.

entries.name: Roots of Evil
entries: As the first Domain of Dread, Barovia might serve an important role in the metaphysical underpinnings of the setting. Some Darklords might have emerged from the Mists first in Barovia, encountered Strahd, and found their ways to their own domains later. Others might have a particular interest in Barovia and in undoing Strahd's connection to his land. Consider these possibilities when developing plots involving conflicts between Darklords or escaping the Mists, as outlined in {@book chapter 3|RHW|3}.

entries.name: Signs of the Count
entries: Servants of Strahd are everywhere in Barovia. While in the domain, characters experience regular reminders that the Darklord watches them—and that they are unwelcome. Roll on the Strahd's Presence table to inspire such encounters.

entries.caption: Strahd's Presence
rows: As clouds momentarily resemble clawed hands, gaping skulls, or Castle Ravenloft.
As wolves or ravens soundlessly follow the characters and mirror their actions.
By faded carvings or tapestries bearing the von Zarovich crest.
During a tarokka reading, when the Darklord card changes to resemble Strahd.
In a dream where a character becomes charmed or threatened by Strahd.
In a rustic shrine littered with vials of dried blood, snapped splinters, and wolf fangs.
When characters awake to find swarms of roosting bats or bloodless animal corpses.
When spying villagers try to eavesdrop on or steal small things from the characters.

entries.name: Barovia Campaign Arc
entries: Campaigns set in Barovia involve communities oppressed by fear of Strahd and his cycles of obsession. A campaign here might follow this outline:

items.name: Levels 1–5
items.entry: The characters emerge from the Mists and seek shelter from danger in the village of Barovia, Krezk, or Vallaki. There they learn the people fear Count von Zarovich, and the characters might meet a {@book reincarnation of Tatyana|RHW|2|Tatyana's Reincarnation}. They help strike back against agents of the count, such as deadly {@creature Wolf|XMM|Wolves} or {@creature Zombie|XMM|Zombies}.

items.name: Levels 6–8
items.entry: The characters learn more of the count's history and about magical relics that might help oppose him. They seek these in haunted corners of the domain, facing servants of Strahd and remnants of his past, including {@creature Ghost|XMM|Ghosts}, {@creature Werewolf|XMM|Werewolves}, and deadly {@creature Druid|XMM|Druids}.

items.name: Levels 9–10
items.entry: Armed with knowledge and magical weapons, the characters lay siege to {@area Castle Ravenloft|0d9|x}, facing {@creature Strahd von Zarovich|RHW|Strahd's} most terrifying servants and the count himself.

entries.name: Blood o' the Vine
entries: {@i Adventure for Level 6 Characters}

items.name: Situation
items.entry: A discovery beneath the Blood of the Vine tavern in the village of Barovia has unleashed sinister magic, transforming the owners.

items.name: Hook
items.entry: Disguised as a human, Urwin Martikov (Medium {@creature Wereraven|RHW}; see {@area chapter 5|3cc|x}) of the Blue Water Inn in Vallaki hires the characters to investigate why the Blood of the Vine tavern in the village of Barovia has closed, claiming he was in negotiations with the owners to buy it. Urwin offers to pay each character 500 GP.

entries.title: The Blood of the Vine tavern is one of the few places in the village of Barovia that welcomes strangers

entries.name: Encounters
entries: This adventure uses {@book map 2.2: Blood of the Vine Tavern|RHW|2|Map 2.2: Blood of the Vine Tavern} and consists of these encounters.

entries.name: Corrupted Owners
entries: The three human sisters who owned the Blood of the Vine tavern—Alenka, Mirabel, and Sorvina—have been transformed into the following ravenous embodiments of primal hunger:
{@b Alenka} ({@creature Relentless Slasher|RHW}) wields kitchenware and prowls the first floor.
{@b Mirabel} (Medium {@creature Ghast Gravecaller|XMM}) stalks the second floor with her two {@creature Ghoul|XMM} servants.
{@b Sorvina} ({@creature Unspeakable Horror|RHW}; see {@area chapter 5|3c4|x}) conducts strange rituals in the basement.

entries.name: Taproom
entries: Dust covers the taproom. Here, the animate corpse of Arik the barkeep mindlessly cleans pieces of shattered glassware at the bar. If attacked or otherwise interrupted, Arik falls to pieces.

entries.name: Office
entries: The office in the northeast corner of the first floor is cluttered with junk and unfinished notes to the count describing the activities of the tavern's patrons. This suggests the owners spied on the locals. If the characters have visited the tavern before, their activities are detailed. A trapdoor descends to an area beneath the tavern.

entries.name: Private Dining Room
entries: The dining room in the southeast corner of the second floor is strewn with broken dishes, rotten food, and pages from a destroyed book. The pages are from the journal of Mirabel, one of the inn's owners. Surviving entries note the following information:

items.name: First Entry
items.entry: Mirabel's sister Sorvina discovered a hollow hidden under the tavern. Strange ruins lie amid the dirt walls. Sorvina wants to excavate them, hoping to find a tomb filed with treasures.

items.name: Second Entry
items.entry: Sorvina's digging revealed strange standing stones. Mirabel worries about her sister, who's started whispering to herself as she obsesses over learning more about the stones.

items.name: Third Entry
items.entry: Sorvina discovered the bones of some monster at the center of the standing stones. All three sisters are having nightmares from which they wake up ravenous. Sorvina claims the hunger is a blessing. Food has lost its taste, but the inn's patrons suddenly smell delicious.

entries.name: Temple of Ravenkind
entries: A circle of ancient menhirs carved with stylized ravens and sunbursts is partially unearthed beneath the tavern. The creature that was once Sorvina is here, chanting over a monstrous skeleton of black bones with stag horns and tusks. When she notices intruders, Sorvina howls, causing the bones to rise as a {@creature Minotaur Skeleton|XMM}. Both it and Sorvina fight the characters to the death. When the skeleton is defeated, its bones remain intact.

entries.name: Cursed Remains
entries: The skeleton exudes evil magic. Casting {@spell Remove Curse|XPHB} on the monster destroys the skeleton. However, if a character touches the skeleton (while it's animate or otherwise), its spirit whispers to the character, bidding them to end its ancient curse. Doing so destroys the skeleton and grants the character the {@feat Second Skin|RHW} Dark Gift. A character who already has a Dark Gift can't accept the curse.

entries.name: Returning to Urwin
entries: If the characters defeat the monsters in the tavern and return to Urwin, he pays them and shares that a benevolent relic, the {@item Holy Symbol of Ravenkind|CoS}, was created at a site like the one hidden under the tavern. The circle was used later to imprison a vicious spirit of the Svalich Woods. If the characters destroyed the skeleton, Urwin rewards them with two random {@item Quaal's Feather Token|XDMG|Quaal's Feather Tokens}. Each character also receives a bonus 1,400 XP.

images.title: Map 2.2: Blood of the Vine Tavern

entries.name: Treacherous Blood
entries: {@i Adventure for Level 9 Characters}

items.name: Situation
items.entry: Lyssa von Zarovich (Medium, Chaotic Evil {@creature Vampire Nightbringer|XMM}), a distant relative of Strahd, plots to undermine the count by unleashing her monstrous servants on Barovia.

items.name: Hook
items.entry: A human resident of Vallaki named Ernst Larnak (Medium, Lawful Evil {@creature Spy|XMM}) tells the characters that locals have seen figures excavating the churchyard in the ruins of Berez. He offers to pay 2,500 GP if they investigate. He doesn't share that his information comes from the count's servants.

entries.name: Encounters
entries: This adventure uses {@book map 2.2: Von Zarovich Sanctuary|RHW|2|Map 2.2: Von Zarovich Sanctuary} and consists of these encounters.

entries.name: Berez Churchyard
entries: A stone slab covering a grave in the churchyard has been shattered, revealing stairs leading down to a von Zarovich sanctuary. There, two {@creature Vampire Spawn|XMM} guardians fight trespassers.

entries.name: Lyssa's Plot
entries: Lyssa greets the characters in the sanctuary's central room. Observing that they don't look like servants of the count, she shares the following information:

items: She is a distant relative of Count Strahd von Zarovich. She, too, is a vampire.
She seeks to depose Strahd and raise Barovia to its deserved glory.
With the aid of allies beyond the Mists, she has created servants immune to many of Strahd's powers. Now she needs to test them.

entries: Regardless of whether the characters agree to aid Lyssa, she opens a door to the adjacent chamber and unleashes a pair of {@creature Vampire Mind Flayer|RHW|Vampire Mind Flayers}. The monsters obey Lyssa's commands and fight the characters. Lyssa fights as well but flees if she takes damage, swearing her revenge.

entries.name: Crypt
entries: The chamber to the north holds the bloodless corpse of a mind flayer and a journal filled with accounts of strange dreams. The journal is a Mist talisman linked to the domain of {@area Bluetspur|2a5|x} (see "{@book Other Domains of Dread|RHW|2}" later in this book).

entries.name: Hidden Chamber
entries: A character who investigates the large room as a {@action Study|XPHB} action and succeeds on a {@dc 16} Intelligence ({@skill Investigation|XPHB}) check finds evidence of a bricked-over hidden chamber in the east wall. Characters can physically remove the wall in 1 hour (half that time if they have Mason's Tools).

entries.name: Elder Evil
entries: Strahd buried a foe in a coffin behind the main chamber's east wall. Opening the coffin releases a {@creature Vampire Nosferatu|RHW} and a {@creature Swarm of Infesting Insects|RHW} (both detailed in {@book chapter 5|RHW|5}) that fight to the death. Also in the coffin is a {@item Gem of Brightness|XDMG}.

entries.name: Conclusion
entries: If the characters defeat or drive off Lyssa, each character gains a bonus 2,600 XP.

entries.name: Borca
entries: {@i Domain of Desire and Deceit}

items.name: Darklords:
items.entry: {@area Ivana Boritsi|0f8|x} and {@area Ivan Dilisnya|0fa|x}

items.name: Genres:
items.entry: {@area Gothic horror|37b|x} and {@area psychological horror|388|x}

items.name: Hallmarks:
items.entry: Political intrigue, poison, revenge

items.name: Mist Talismans:
items.entry: Dram of sweet-smelling poison, flame-singed love letter, tarnished signet ring

items.name: Aligned Tarokka Card:
items.entry: Beggar

items.name: Opposed Tarokka Card:
items.entry: Tempter

entries: The noble families of Borca engage in an endless war of conspiracies and lethal betrayals as they pursue influence, thrills, and age-old rivalries. People here value guile and callousness, and none embody those traits better than the domain's two Darklords: the genius poisoner {@creature Ivana Boritsi|RHW} and the childishly cruel stalker {@creature Ivan Dilisnya|RHW}.

Beyond the fashionable holdings of Borca's elite, commoners struggle against crime, poverty, and starvation. In scattered villages and tenement-filled cities, locals view nobles as celebrities, and the commoners' idealized vision of noble life leads them to mimic the aristocrats' callousness and appetite for empty fads. Desperate commoners are tempted into debt and depravity in pursuit of nobles' favor and passing fancies. Those who don't bend to the whims of Borca's elite face humiliation before they're crushed.

entries.name: Domain Features
entries: Borca is known for the following features:

items.name: Two Darklords
items.entry: {@area Ivana Boritsi|0f8|x} and {@area Ivan Dilisnya|0fa|x} are both Darklords of Borca. They control the domain equally—one of countless facts that contributes to their mutual loathing.

items.name: Cruel Intentions
items.entry: Borca's most influential families are vicious rivals bound by blood, politics, and business. Their rivalries unfold in an endless shadow war of lies, shifting alliances, and assassinations. The nobles value appearances above all else; they favor stylish poisons, clever clockwork devices, and public embarrassment in their plots.

items.name: Desperate Sycophants
items.entry: Commoners own little, renting homes in crowded cities or rural villages that serve noble interests. Many commoners desperately court the favor of the elite.

items.name: Noble Intrigue
items.entry: Nobles control all aspects of Borcan society. They squabble over artistic, political, and religious influence to feed their gluttonous egos and fill their idle lives. The Boritsi family holds the greatest influence.

entries.name: Locations in Borca
entries: The impoverished lands of Borca languish in the shadow of opulent noble estates and garish monuments to greed. The exploited land bears the scars of age-old rivalries and the hiding places of noble secrets.
The capital of Levkarest is described below, and other notable locations are summarized on the {@book Borca Locations table|RHW|2|Borca Locations}.

entries.name: Levkarest
entries: Borca's capital of Levkarest stands at the pinnacle of culture and ostentatious power. Here, lesser nobles pursue fickle dramas and vogue trends while the populace labors amid workhouses and cultlike fashion houses. Commoners glimpse this world of toxic glamor only from afar or when nobles exploit street-level fads.

At the city's heart rises the People's Parliament, the land's ineffective seat of government. Here, unpopular members of noble families squabble over meaningless legislation. The nation's true rulers conduct business on noble estates, in avant-garde clubs, and in the offices of faceless mercantile ventures.

Near the People's Parliament stands the Great Cathedral. Within, pious commoners and ostentatious aristocrats attend ceremonies sacred to the deity Ezra. Locals say the cathedral was created by nobles who believed they'd earned the ire of some great evil. Centuries later, these families still support the church, transactionally investing in dubious supernatural insurance.

entries.caption: Borca Locations
rows: No one knows who built the cursed mansion of Alichino's Hall, but tales claim that devils gather here nightly to gamble. Allegedly anyone who bests the cheating devils is granted a wish.
The abandoned House of Lament abuts the tower of a ruined castle. Numerous spirits haunt this accursed place, which has been known to appear in the Mists and in other domains.
The residents of Lechburg pretend not to notice that many of their neighbors are clockwork creations of Ivan Dilisnya, fearing their unpredictable lord's reprisal if they don't play along.
Owned by the Ocrotire family, Leoni has a baseless reputation for harboring bandits. Zealous circuit judges from Sturben often visit and arrest those they deem suspicious.
After Ilvin burned, the residents rebuilt their homes to the east. The people of New Ilvin never speak of the tragedy that befell them and constructed a new road to avoid Old Ilvin.
The homes of Borca's most influential families are detailed in "{@area Nobles of Borca|103|x}."
Only ruins remain of the town of Ilvin. Hidden within sealed cellars lie the charred remains of many who now appear to reside in the town of New Ilvin.
Poisonous fungi and plants grow in the Poison Orchard—the deadliest grow at the forest's heart. Several noble-backed companies use these plants as secret ingredients in their products.
Scions of Borca's noble families viciously compete to attend the elite Scholomance school of magic. The school is home to the Rainmaker Society, which is said to control Borcan politics.
Sturben is home to the Faceless Court, in which groups of five masked judges execute severe justice across social classes. Their iron-masked circuit judges travel widely.
A hound-like horror appears nightly to terrorize the village of Vor Ziyden. Only one local knows the monster seeks the corpse of its brother, who died near a druidic site in the nearby woods.

entries.name: Ivana Boritsi
entries: {@i Darklord of Poisonous Ambition}
Your kind can't possibly fathom how much this sort of interruption costs the House of Boritsi. Don't make it worse by being tedious.

Ivana Boritsi tires of playing games no one realizes she's already won. The head of Borca's most influential family, Ivana effortlessly manipulates all aspects of society. Her servants, employees, and fractious cousins prove endlessly unreliable, forcing her to attend personally to tedious duties and social functions to maintain her power.

Ivana looks eternally youthful, causing some to doubt her competence and attribute her success to her elder cousin, {@creature Ivan Dilisnya|RHW}. Perpetually bored with and disappointed by predictable people, she takes refuge in her alchemical studies and her gardens of poisonous plants. Her vast knowledge of toxins makes her a peerless poisoner. She uses her creations to make people more pliable and to assassinate those she deems too boorish to live. Secretly, Ivana seeks someone who can match her intellect and relieve her boredom, but she quickly grows weary of even the most charming and clever souls.

entries.name: Ivana's Tale
entries: The firstborn of a fantastically wealthy family, Ivana Boritsi was clever, poised, and—in the eyes of her father, Klaus—utterly unfit to lead the family. Driven to prove her father wrong and encouraged by her mother, Camille, Ivana learned every aspect of her family's vast perfume and medicine business.

Her focus on her goals faltered when she met Pieter, a skilled chemist in her family's service. Camille recognized her daughter's budding romance and saw the lowborn Pieter as a threat to the girl's prospects. Camille seduced Pieter and arranged for her daughter to discover them, intent on convincing her to forsake this and all future romances.

Ivana took a different lesson from the experience. Realizing the depths of her family's depravity, she poisoned her mother and her callous younger siblings with toxins of her own design. None suspected Ivana, but soon after, Klaus summoned her. Having lost his sons, Klaus named her cousin, Ivan Dilisnya, his heir. Ivana was the last to be informed, her father having already announced his decision to the estate and sent a messenger to inform the Dilisnyas.

Having expected this, Ivana had poisoned the perfumery on the estate's grounds, turning its far-ranging smells deadly. Ivana laughed as toxic mist poured across the estate. As she sipped the antidote, she watched Klaus and the manor's staff die choking amid a violet fog. When the poison cleared, the Mists had surrounded the land of Borca (for details on Ivana's {@area domain|0fc|x} and {@area lair|0fd|x}, see "{@area Ivan Dilisnya|0fa|x}").

entries.name: Ivan Dilisnya
entries: {@i Darklord of Childish Cruelty}
Oh ho oh! What succulent insipidity I see in those lumpish eyes! What divine folly! I swoon, I croon, I know! You'll be a new favorite!

Ivan Dilisnya—a spoiled, selfish attention seeker—has the tastes of a child but a withered wreck of a body. Borcan society widely regards this recluse as an innovator and a genius. In truth, he is neither. Ivan is a wicked and off-putting manipulator who, despite his appearance and intellect, affects childlike behaviors and speech patterns for attention. He is alone—despite having been born with everything—and the solitude terrifies him.

By staying out of sight or using deceptive correspondence, Ivan makes his targets doubt reality and overestimate his control. He manipulates people into performing pointless errands for him or spying on his cousin, {@creature Ivana Boritsi|RHW}, whom he believes mocks him and has caused his aged, squalid state.

Ivan surrounds himself with dangerous clockwork toys and fawning, mechanical servants. While he goes through the motions of crafting such devices, the Dark Powers provide him with any creation he desires, no matter how bizarre.

entries.name: Ivan's Tale
entries: Treachery is tradition for the Dilisnya family. The land of Borca originates from the same world as Barovia's conquerors, the von Zaroviches. Dilisnyas were at Castle Ravenloft the night of Strahd von Zarovich's transformation.

Ages later, the siblings Boris and Camille Dilisnya plotted to steal the prestige of their cousins, the Boritsis. Camille manipulated her way into the Boritsi family, while Boris prepared his son, Ivan, to act as head of a united Boritsi–Dilisnya house.

Despite abundant opportunities, Ivan resented his family's plans for him and grew to embody puerile vice and impulses. His family's wealth and doting servants exacerbated this problem, even as Ivan fixated on childish toys and behaviors. Frequent "accidents" transpired around him, forcing his family to cover up the maimings and deaths of numerous pets and servants. Only his beloved sister, Kristina, remained immune to his wicked tricks. Although Ivan's family grew unsettled by Ivan, they sheltered the future head of their house, reshaping their estate and investing in whimsical diversions for him.

On the same night Ivana Boritsi poisoned her family, Ivan learned his parents intended to send Kristina to a prestigious boarding school. Screams, cruel cackling, and clockwork screeching filled the estate that night. When it ended, Ivan Dilisnya was the last living member of his family, and the Mists had closed around Borca.

entries.name: Ivana and Ivan's Domain
entries: Borca is altered by Ivana's and Ivan's presences, creating the following effects:

items.name: Closing the Borders
items.entry: While in their domain, Ivana or Ivan can close or open the borders to Borca at will. While the borders are closed, the Mists surrounding Borca are filled with briar vines (Ivana) or barbed wire (Ivan). The Mists become {@variantrule Difficult Terrain|XPHB}, and whenever a creature moves into or within the Mists, it takes {@damage 2d4} Piercing damage for every 5 feet it travels. Once Ivana or Ivan closes or opens the borders, neither Darklord can do so again until the next dawn.

items.name: Ivana's Whispers (Ivana Only)
items.entry: Ivana can encode a message of 25 words or fewer into flowers in Borca at dawn. Until the next dawn, the first time a creature smells a flower, it hears the message in its mind, understanding the meaning of the message.

items.name: Mystery Letters (Ivan Only)
items.entry: Whenever Ivan writes a letter and addresses it to a creature in Borca whose name he knows and that he's seen before, the letter appears near that creature at the next dawn. The letter appears in an unobserved but obvious space, such as a bedside table or among gear.

items.name: Spies
items.entry: While in Borca, Ivana and Ivan can cast {@spell Clairvoyance|XPHB}, requiring no Material components and using Intelligence as the spellcasting ability. To cast the spell, there must be a Large or bigger plant (Ivana) or a Tiny doll or similar toy (Ivan) within range from which the sensor appears.

entries: If a Darklord of Borca dies, that Darklord's effects end immediately. Those effects resume if that Darklord gains a new body (see {@creature Ivana Boritsi|RHW|Ivana's} and {@creature Ivan Dilisnya|RHW|Ivan's} Darklord Restoration traits).

entries.name: Ivana's Lair
entries: The vast Boritsi estate, Misericordia, hosts fields, greenhouses, and alchemy labs used to create Boritsi-branded perfumes and tinctures. Within the manor, the Darklord cultivates rare and unique plants in greenhouse-like conservatory wings only she can enter.

entries.name: Misericordia Conservatories
entries: The conservatories of Misericordia, the Boritsi estate, hold strange plants, many of which Ivana cultivated. One such site is depicted on map 2.4 and has the following features:

items.name: Gardens
items.entry: Plants of exceptional beauty and toxicity flourish under Ivana's care in Misericordia's gardens and greenhouses. Many require unusual fertilizers to flourish—particularly the carnivorous plants.

items.name: Laboratory
items.entry: In her lab, the Darklord brews deadly alchemical concoctions and wondrous perfumes. A sizable perfume organ here is filled with rare scents and lethal poisons.

items.name: Nursery
items.entry: Hidden behind a rotating wall is Ivana's private nursery and study. Here she cultivates new plant species, many of which have supernatural properties or monstrous life.

entries.name: Ivan's Lair
entries: The Dilisnya estate, Degravo, is a confusing tangle of topiary gardens, hedge mazes, and neglected petting zoos that abut sheer cliffs. At the heart of the grounds sits a largely abandoned compound that includes the Laughing House, Ivan's home.

entries.name: The Laughing House
entries: The only occupied structure in Degravo, the Laughing House has been renovated and redecorated to suit a child's whimsical tastes. The site is depicted on map 2.4 and has the following features:

items.name: Ivan's Playroom
items.entry: On the second floor of the manor, a gallery of childish art leads to doors minded by a clockwork attendant that Ivan can taunt intruders through. At the hall's end is a fake bedroom decorated for a neglected youngster. A hidden door there leads to Ivan's true personal chambers. This playroom is a wonderland of ingenious toys, all of which are secretly deadly. Beyond the Darklord's personal chamber lie secret rooms where he creates new playthings and discards toys he's grown tired of.

items.name: Playhouse
items.entry: Beyond a lobby attended by clockwork staff, a theater hosts brutal stage productions for Ivan's amusement. Performers might be life-size toys or desperate prisoners.

entries.name: Adventures in Borca
entries: Adventures set in Borca involve intrigue between petty nobles, family secrets, manipulation, and deception. Use this section to develop horror adventures in this domain.

entries.title: At Misericordia, the Boritsi family estate, Ivana Boritsi indulges her research into rare and deadly plants

entries.name: Horror in Borca
entries: Adventures in Borca often include the following elements.

entries.name: Nobles of Borca
entries: Twelve depraved families rule over every aspect of life in Borca. Each is a core family of fifteen or fewer irredeemable individuals united around the head of the house, who tyrannically controls the clan's myriad ventures. Servants and business representatives across the domain support these core families, as do members of branch families—lackeys with different surnames who are distantly related to the core family or indentured to them.
The Noble Families of Borca table details the domain's most powerful families.

entries.name: Supernatural Intrigue
entries: Magic and the supernatural heighten the gilded depravities of Borca. When planning adventures in Borca, consider including the following elements.

entries.name: Curses and Poison
entries: {@book Chapter 3|XDMG|2} of the {@book Dungeon Master's Guide|XDMG} describes curses, magical contagions, and poison. Villains in Borca might use these or adjusted versions of your own design—perhaps with signature cosmetic effects.

entries.name: Monsters
entries: Monsters can serve as threats but also agents of noble houses. {@variantrule Shape-shifting|XPHB} creatures such as {@creature Imp|XMM|Imps} and {@creature Wererat|XMM|Wererats} excel at infiltration, while {@creature Doppelganger|XMM|Doppelgangers} and {@creature Succubus|XMM|Succubi} might pose as nobles, innocents, or trusted allies. Creatures like {@creature Ghost|XMM|Ghosts} and {@creature Intellect Devourer|XMM|Intellect Devourers} might control individuals, making them behave in unusual ways. Choose a single monster, and consider how you might make it central to a devious noble's plot.

entries.name: Spells and Magic Items
entries: Don't try to account for or incorporate all magic into adventures involving supernatural intrigue. Choose a single spell—such as {@spell Clairvoyance|XPHB}, {@spell Geas|XPHB}, or {@spell Speak with Dead|XPHB}—and consider how to make it central to a plot involving secrets and mysterious revelations. Consider doing the same with a magic item such as a {@item Cloak of the Bat|XDMG}, a {@item Hag Eye|XDMG}, or {@item Sending Stones|XDMG}. Characters should earn rewards for using their own magic in clever ways.

entries.name: Borca Campaign Arc
entries: Campaigns set in Borca typically involve the domain's noble families. A campaign here might follow this outline:

items.name: Levels 1–3
items.entry: A scheming noble targets the characters, presenting them with an opportunity or exorbitant compensation for a simple task. In the aftermath, the characters discover they have given up something of great value or realize they look like criminals.

items.name: Levels 4–6
items.entry: The scheming noble or a rival offers the characters a way out of their predicament, but this involves a dangerous confrontation or daring infiltration. Supernatural forces—such as jilted doppelgangers, cursed family members, or dead nobility—might aid the characters. Against expectations, the characters complete their mission and turn the tables on their foe.

items.name: Levels 7–8
items.entry: The characters' activities attract the attention of {@creature Ivana Boritsi|RHW} or {@creature Ivan Dilisnya|RHW}. The Darklord invites the characters to their estate or corresponds with them from afar, using them as agents in their rivalry. For example, either Darklord might task the characters with seeking out the missing will of Ivana's father, Klaus.

entries.name: Lethal Lavender
entries: {@i Adventure for Level 5 Characters}

items.name: Situation
items.entry: The human siblings Eroso and Siobhan Manduchi (Medium, Neutral Evil {@creature Noble|XMM|Nobles}) are scions of a minor noble family who stole a rare plant, not realizing it belonged to the Boritsis. After discovering the owner, they feign their deaths to avoid reprisal.

items.name: Hook
items.entry: Eroso and Siobhan seek out the characters to use as patsies in their staged murder.

entries.name: Encounters
entries: The adventure consists of these encounters.

entries.name: Dig Your Vibe
entries: Eroso and Siobhan Manduchi approach the characters and invite them to go carousing if the adventurers agree to share tales of their exploits. The two are {@variantrule Friendly [Attitude]|XPHB|Friendly} and flattering.

entries.name: Exquisite Corpse
