The eerie dummy has a mind of its own, insisting it is the real Strahd and that the creature currently sitting on the throne of Castle Ravenloft is a mere impostor. It rages at the audience and makes audacious promises to any who will help it regain its station. Anyone with the slightest inkling of who Strahd is finds this hilarious. Their peals of laughter are agony to Doerdon's ears.

entries.name: Scaena

items.name: Darklord:
items.entry: Lemont Sediam Juste

items.name: Hallmarks:
items.entry: Reality-manipulating theater

entries: Lemont Sediam Juste fancied himself a serious playwright, and he achieved popular, if not critical, acclaim in {@book Dementlieu|RHW|2} for his works of grisly horror. But he craved respectability, and with his new play {@i Apparitions}, Lemont believed he would find it. The night of the premiere, when the audience signaled their boredom, the playwright was crestfallen. His supporters wanted blood, so he gave them what they craved.

By the play's end, Lemont had joined the drama and brutally murdered every member of the cast while the crowd roared their approval. As the show ended, the playhouse broke from Dementlieu, and Scaena was formed. Comprising a single playhouse, the domain can create any reality Lemont desires on its stage. The Darklord's immersive performances become somewhat predictable, though, as they always end in slaughter.

entries.name: Sea of Sorrows

items.name: Darklord:
items.entry: Pietra van Riese

items.name: Hallmarks:
items.entry: Island domains, nautical horror

entries: The murderous pirate Pietra van Riese, captain of the {@i Relentless}, had an unsavory reputation for attaching her captives to ropes and dragging them through the water until they drowned. She never removed the detritus of her victims, even though some returned to life as zombies. Rival captains ultimately sank the {@i Relentless}, but death couldn't keep Pietra. She awoke in the Sea of Sorrows, water in her lungs and sea creatures making their homes in her flesh. Her crew stirred with her, now fish-eaten corpses. When the captain sought to speak with them, her voice emerged from their mouths.

The {@i Relentless} sails a domain that can overlap any body of water in any other domain. Some domains border the Sea of Sorrows, while others call these mysterious waters by other names. Those who venture into the Mists by ship might enter the sea's endless debris-choked expanse of eerie beasts and shifting islands, including the following:

items.name: Blaustein
items.entry: The island-fortress domain known as Blaustein once was ruled by the notorious Bluebeard, but his spectral wives overthrew him and now endlessly torment him.

items.name: Dominia
items.entry: The asylum of the vampire Dr. Daclaud Heinfroth rises up from the stormy island of Dominia. The asylum's patients are all dramatically different versions of Heinfroth, inspired by who he was at various points during his lengthy life.

items.name: Isle of the Ravens
items.entry: A storm of ravens surrounds this forested islet. Hidden amid the feathered gale, an impossibly tall tower reaches into the sky. The Isle of Ravens is home to the Lady of Ravens, and any who offend her join her ebon-winged flock.

items.name: The Lighthouse
items.entry: Otherworldly light shines from the Lighthouse, a twisted spur of bizarre fossils. Anyone who enters finds that instead of broadcasting a warning across the sea, the light calls down to what lurks in the pit hidden beneath it.

items.name: Vigilant's Bluff
items.entry: An undead paladin holds vigil atop Vigilant's Bluff. Weary travelers can find refuge here if they respect the paladin's faith. The bones of those who don't litter the surrounding coral.

entries.name: Souragne

items.name: Darklord:
items.entry: Anton Misroi

items.name: Hallmarks:
items.entry: Imprisonment, swamp magic

entries: In society, Anton Misroi presented himself as an upstanding gentleman who served as a prison warden. But within the walls of the prison, he became a sadist who believed righteousness was on his side. When his torturous punishments finally drove the inmates to rise up, the bloody riot that ensued drew the attentions of the Dark Powers. During the uprising, Anton drowned in the swamps surrounding the prison. But he rose again soon after, an undead warden in search of inmates.

Beyond Anton's prison, alligator-filled swamps cover the domain of Souragne right up to the sinking settlement of Port d'Elhour and Marais d'Tarascon, a village where above-ground crypts outnumber the residences of the living.

entries.title: In Souragne, the walking dead rise to terrorize the village of Marais d'Tarascon

entries.name: Staunton Bluffs

items.name: Darklord:
items.entry: Teresa Bleysmith

items.name: Hallmarks:
items.entry: Endless warfare, repeating history

entries: Eerily faceless mercenary regiments sweep the countryside of Staunton Bluffs. Burning villages and killing helpless residents, they push ever eastward toward Castle Stonecrest, hereditary home of the Bleysmith family. Teresa Bleysmith, spurred by jealousy of her brother, Torrence, gave her family's foes the intelligence they needed to raid Staunton Bluffs. The attack was never supposed to go so far, but the duplicitous mercenaries stormed through the countryside to take Castle Stonecrest within a day. Teresa survived the attack—but when she surveyed the damage done and found her family dead by her own designs, she threw herself from the bluffs. Now she haunts her own domain, where she repeatedly relives the day of her betrayal.

entries.name: Tovag

items.name: Darklord:
items.entry: Kas the Bloody Handed

items.name: Hallmarks:
items.entry: Undead military dictatorship

entries: Notorious across the planes, the vampire Kas was once the champion of the lich {@book Vecna|XDMG|8|Vecna}. Wielding the mighty sword that bears his name, he betrayed his master, and the resulting battle supposedly destroyed them both. In truth, Vecna escaped and grew in power over ages and across worlds. Kas, though, was claimed by the Mists, and in his wasteland domain of Tovag, he believes his war with Vecna rages on. Patrols of prisoner-soldiers under undead commanders scour the land, dragooning strangers to serve in Kas's armies and to manufacture bizarre war machines. When Kas deems the time right, he sends his forces into the Mists, believing that Vecna's realm lies just beyond. Invariably, those troops never return, leaving the vampire to rage, rebuild his forces, and continue his search for the {@item Sword of Kas|XDMG} ({@book detailed|XDMG|6|Magic Items A-Z} in the {@book Dungeon Master's Guide|XDMG}), which he considers his key to victory.

At least once, the Dark Powers have released Kas from his domain to torment their onetime prisoner Vecna. Inevitably, when Kas fails to defeat his ancient rival, the Mists return him to his domain to rage for another era.

entries.name: Zherisia

items.name: Darklord:
items.entry: Sodo

items.name: Hallmarks:
items.entry: Serial murderers, urban decay

entries: Each day, the city of Paridon in Zherisia erupts in riots over food scarcity, taxation, and the disappearance of dozens of citizens every night. At least one murderer stalks Paridon: the ancient doppelganger Sodo, which has impersonated so many people that it now finds it impossible to hold a form for more than a few days at a time. As Sodo's flesh runs like hot wax, it staves off dissolution by consuming the organs of humanoids. Paridon's streets serve as the Darklord's hunting ground, and those who enter the sewers risk attracting the notice of countless insectile horrors, including {@creature Carrion Stalker|RHW|carrion stalkers} (see {@area chapter 5|39a|x}) and their monstrous Hive Queen.

entries.name: Random Domains
entries: Characters might find themselves in an unexpected domain when the Mists initially carry them to the Domains of Dread or if they aimlessly enter the Mists. When this occurs, roll on or choose a result from the Random Domains of Dread table to determine where the characters emerge. Domains marked with an asterisk (*) are featured domains detailed earlier in this book.

The Random Domains of Dread table also lists what tarokka cards are aligned with and opposed to each domain. Cards marked with a dagger (†) are also aligned with another Domain of Dread. Randomly determine which domain the Mists transport characters to when this card is drawn.

data.name: Chapter 3: Ravenloft Adventures

entries.title: Alanik Ray and Arthur Sedgwick investigate the occult art collection of the Marquis Stezen d'Polarno

entries: Drafting frightful encounters, adventures, and campaigns requires deliberate choices that cultivate an atmosphere of dread. This chapter explores how you, the DM, can use the Domains of Dread to create suspense during play and craft terrifying adventures.

entries.name: Campaigns in the Mists
entries: The surreal natures of the Domains of Dread, their Darklords, the Mists, and the Dark Powers serve as tools in your frightful adventures. None of them are too important to use, too mysterious to reveal, or too fragile to pit against characters. This section explores how to use the most infamous features of the Domains of Dread to create frightful campaigns.
Before preparing horror adventures, review the "{@book Ensuring Fun for All|XDMG|0|Ensuring Fun for All}" section of the {@book Dungeon Master's Guide|XDMG}.

entries.name: Creating a Horror Campaign
entries: When planning campaigns in Ravenloft, consider the guidance in {@book chapter 5|XDMG|4} of the {@book Dungeon Master's Guide|XDMG}, along with the following topics. Record the answers to the questions in this section to inspire your campaign.

entries.name: Campaign Characters
entries: Characters exploring the Mists are the protagonists of horror stories. {@book Chapter 1|RHW|1} provides advice for players creating such characters, but you should also consider the following questions:

items.name: Character Origins
items.entry: Do the characters originate from the Domains of Dread, or were they abducted from somewhere else?

items.name: Context for Surreality
items.entry: Do the characters realize something is strange about the Domains of Dread, or is the Land of the Mists all they've ever known?

items.name: Scale of Experience
items.entry: Do the characters know about a single domain—perhaps one they all call home—or do they have firsthand experience with multiple domains or other settings?

items.name: Characters' Significance
items.entry: Why are the characters special? Why did the Dark Powers, a Darklord, or something else take note of them?

items.name: Consistency of Experience
items.entry: Do the answers to these questions apply to all of the characters?

entries.name: Campaign Themes
entries: Explore the types of horror adventures you're interested in by considering the following questions:

items.name: Horror Inspirations
items.entry: What horror stories or media inspire your campaign?

items.name: Horror Icons
items.entry: Is there a classic monster or villain that inspires the foes in your campaign?

items.name: Horror Genres
items.entry: What genres of horror—detailed in {@area chapter 4|34d|x} or elsewhere—does your campaign focus on?

items.name: Domains of Dread
items.entry: Which Domains of Dread or Darklords do you want to focus on? Or do you want to create a new {@area Domain of Dread|347|x} and {@area Darklord|339|x} following the methods in chapter 4?

entries.name: Setting Mysteries
entries: The Domains of Dread function in unique ways, but if and how that matters is up to you. "{@area Ravenloft Setting Models|2cb|x}" later in this chapter details ways to shape the setting to suit your adventures. Review that section and the following questions as you consider how the nature of the Domains of Dread might shape your adventures:

items.name: Escape
items.entry: Do you want characters to pursue escape from the Domains of Dread? Do they want to leave even if a domain has been their home?

items.name: Islands or Regions
items.entry: Does your campaign focus on a single domain, or are there multiple domains you want to explore?

items.name: Metaphysical Scale
items.entry: Do you want your campaign to explore the unusual nature of the Domains of Dread, or is that nature broadly accepted reality?

items.name: Stories and Secrets
items.entry: Are tales of other domains or settings treated as facts, lore known only to a few, legends, or nonsense? Is that true for all places beyond a domain's borders?

items.name: Traveling the Mists
items.entry: Do people travel between domains? Is travel limited to wanderers such as Vistani, or are tools such as Mist talismans (see the {@area introduction|008|x}) common enough to allow for broader travel and trade?

items.name: Witnesses to the Surreal
items.entry: How do domain residents react to supernatural events? Are they shocked, do they treat them as commonplace, or do they rationalize (or deny) abject horrors?

entries: The answers to these questions are the ones that inspire you and your players.

entries.name: Darklord Conflicts
entries: Each Domain of Dread is a reflection of and a prison for a Darklord. The tales and torments of those Darklords are fundamental to adventures within their borders. Consider the following questions when developing your campaign:

items.name: Darklord Plots
items.entry: Will your campaign involve the history, torment, and potential defeat of a Darklord? Or would you rather keep a Darklord in the background and focus on other stories?

items.name: Darklord Downfalls
items.entry: Can a Darklord be defeated, or are they an enduring and recurring threat? What happens when a Darklord dies?

items.name: Darklord Defiance
items.entry: What does it mean to best a Darklord? Does it mean slaying them, forcing them to open their domain's borders, undermining their plans, or something else?

items.name: Darklord Relationships
items.entry: Does the Darklord want to destroy, conscript, or use the characters? Does the Darklord appear in person or communicate via proxies?

items.name: Darklord Rivalries
items.entry: Does your campaign feature interaction between multiple Darklords? If so, how do they know about each other? What do they have to gain from their conflicts?

entries: The "{@area Using Darklords|2da|x}" section later in this chapter further explores ways to implement Darklords in your campaign.

entries.name: Starting Your Campaign
entries: Start your campaign in a way that establishes an ominous tone or highlights the nature of the Domains of Dread. Consider the following questions:

items.name: Collective Misfortune
items.entry: Do the characters gather to mourn the death of a loved one? Does fate bring them together as the survivors of a disaster, such as a shipwreck or massacre?

items.name: Darklord's Summons
items.entry: When a Darklord calls, the beckoned answer. Do the characters meet after being hired by a Darklord to perform some task or by attending a social event that turns ominous?

items.name: Present Terror
items.entry: Do the characters meet amid terrifying circumstances, such as being prisoners or experiment subjects?

items.name: Shared Mystery
items.entry: Do the characters gather due to a shared mystery? Are they led to the same place by an enigmatic message, or do they start cooperating as they flee being accused of murder?

items.name: Strangers in the Mists
items.entry: Are the characters united by the Mists? Might they be traveling to other destinations when the Mists unite them?

entries: During character creation, you can work with players to come up with reasons why the characters might be in a grim or unusual situation.

A horrific origin might also unite the party. Players might be interested in their characters sharing a species or a Dark Gift from {@book chapter 1|RHW|1}. A group composed entirely of Reborn or of characters who have the Aberrant Anatomy feat can provide ample inspiration for frightful adventures.

entries.name: Campaign Considerations
entries: Think about how you might reinforce a frightful mood over the course of your campaign. Consider the following questions about topics likely to occur during a series of adventures:

items.name: Allies and Sanctuaries
items.entry: Is it ever appropriate to harm characters' allies or destroy places they consider safe? Is doing so worth making players feel like they've lost something they've invested in? See "{@book Tragic Limits|XDMG|0|Tragic Limits}" in the {@book Dungeon Master's Guide|XDMG} for advice on handling these topics.

items.name: Altered States
items.entry: How might characters change during a campaign? Do you want to encourage players to develop character changes resulting from their experiences—such as scars, phobias, or {@area Dark Gifts|0a7|x}?

items.name: Character Secrets
items.entry: What sorts of secrets might a character have, and are those boons to the game or potential disruptions? You can use the advice in the {@book Dungeon Master's Guide|XDMG} on evil characters to incorporate characters with secrets into your game.

items.name: Death
items.entry: What happens when a character dies? How might a character brought back from death be changed?

items.name: Fear
items.entry: How do you expect characters to respond to frightful situations? Is fear reflected only by the {@condition Frightened|XPHB} condition, or would you like players to roleplay shock and revulsion?

items.name: Hope
items.entry: How will you give characters hope and ensure they don't feel like the campaign is pointless? What rays of light perforate the darkness?

items.name: New Characters
items.entry: If a character dies and isn't raised, how will you add a player's new character to the game? Do the Mists reveal a new hero, or might someone in a domain be inspired to act by another's sacrifice?

entries: When thinking about the answers to these questions, avoid changing the rules of the game—these should remain the stable, agreed-on underpinnings of the game.

entries.name: Ending Your Campaign
entries: Horror stories often don't end well, but yours might be an exception. Have an idea of what makes a satisfying end for your horror campaign. Frame your thoughts on this by considering these questions:

items.name: Escape
items.entry: Is the goal of your campaign for the characters to escape a domain or all of the Domains of Dread? Can they achieve this through the defeat of a Darklord, the will of the Dark Powers, or some other magical method?

items.name: Memorial
items.entry: If a character dies, can they still contribute to a campaign's culmination? Can an accidental death become a meaningful sacrifice? Is there important work only a spirit can do?

items.name: Survival
items.entry: Do you expect any or all of the characters to survive the campaign? Will the players be satisfied if their characters' stories end in defeat, transformation, or willing sacrifice?

items.name: Worthy Cause
items.entry: What's the goal the characters might risk everything to achieve? Is this protecting a person or place, or is it keeping something out of a Darklord's hands? Is there a status quo that's vital to restore or a wrong that needs to be undone?

entries: Ensure you and the players agree on what makes a fulfilling end to a campaign. Don't work toward a climax demanding a dramatic sacrifice that no one's interested in making.

entries.name: Further Development
entries: Review your answers to the questions from this section to see what patterns emerge and what answers surprised or excited you. Let those answers develop into ideas for unique horror adventures and arcs for your own campaigns. Next, review the following sections in this chapter. These explore ways to employ elements of the Domains of Dread in your horror adventures and outline whole frightful campaign arcs.

entries.name: Using Domains of Dread
entries: Domains of Dread are backdrops to horror tales and the torments of their Darklord. These domains exist to confound and terrify those within. Everything in a Domain of Dread serves frightful ends, and if something seems like it doesn't, you just haven't revealed how yet. While creating horror adventures, consider the following facts about domains.

entries.name: Ravenloft Setting Models
entries: What domains compose the Ravenloft setting is up to you. The Domains of Dread have no definitive map or proscribed orientation for how they relate to one another. This allows you to shape the setting however best serves your adventures. Consider the following setting models for how you might use the Domains of Dread or use your favorite elements from multiple domains.

entries.name: Haunted House Domain
entries: In this model, a single domain functions like a haunted house. Characters enter, terrifying forces trap them, and the party struggles to escape. This model features in the adventures {@i I6: Ravenloft} (1983) and its descendant {@adventure Curse of Strahd|CoS} (2016). This setting model excels if you want to...

items: Explore the tale of a single domain or Darklord.
Highlight a narrow band of horror genres.
Avoid interacting with multiple domains or traveling the Mists.
Make a Darklord your adventures' focal villain.

entries.name: Islands of Terror
entries: In this model, the Domains of Dread are isolated realms that characters might reach by braving the Mists. Domains' inhabitants might be aware of each other, but travel is uncommon and dangerous. Characters might be inhabitants of a domain or wayward souls from other realms, and events might lead them through the Mists to other nightmarish lands. This model is broadly used in this book. This setting model excels if you want to...

items: Highlight the mystery and surreal nature of the Domains of Dread.
Feature tours of multiple domains while controlling where the Mists lead.
Explore stories focused on the nature of the Mists and escaping the Domains of Dread.
Make Darklords part of a cast of villains.

entries.name: Clusters and Cores
entries: In this model, the Domains of Dread are similar to countries on globe. The Mists mark the edges of the world, rise when Darklords close their domains' borders, and appear as they please, but otherwise domains are separated by geographical and political borders. Domain inhabitants know about one another, travel, and trade, while Darklords are powerful figures that might have political or warlike aspirations. Whether you create a cluster out of a small group of your favorite domains or meld many (or all) the domains into a continent-sized core is up to you. This is the model largely used in earlier explorations of the setting, including {@i Ravenloft: Realm of Terror} (1990) and {@i Domains of Dread} (1997). This setting model excels if you want to...

items: Encourage characters to roam between domains.
Highlight villains who are infamous in multiple domains or who must be pursued (or evaded).
Explore large-scale plots, such as wars between or the destruction of the Domains of Dread.
Obscure Darklords, making them figures that might not be widely known.

entries.name: Gothic Earth
entries: The similarities between {@area Strahd von Zarovich|0d5|x} and Dracula, between {@area Viktra Mordenheim|1f9|x} and Victor Frankenstein, and between other Ravenloft villains and icons of horror fiction and film are intentional. Because many of Ravenloft's villains and domains have real-world roots, elements from Ravenloft are readily portable into frightful adventures set on a version of Earth steeped in magic and gothic horror. While the details of such historic horror are beyond the scope of this book, consider using the tools herein to pit characters against menaces from your favorite horror tales. This model is explored in {@i Ravenloft: Masque of the Red Death and Other Tales} (1994).

entries.name: Domain Details
entries: To reinforce the types of horror your adventures explore and give domains their own malicious personalities, consider these setting elements.

entries.title: Those who enter the Mists...

entries.name: Darklord Reflections
entries: Every domain can be viewed through the lens of its Darklord, with every part of the domain reflecting some facet of the Darklord's personality, ambition, or torment. You might think about adventures in a domain as explorations of a Darklord's identity.

For example, {@book Barovia's|RHW|2} gothic horror themes include deception, oppression, and predation, all of which are aspects of the domain's Darklord, {@creature Strahd von Zarovich|RHW}. While the village of Vallaki leans toward themes of deception, the village of Barovia, in the shadow of Castle Ravenloft, is steeped in oppression. The domain's deadly wildernesses and beasts reinforce themes of predation. When a domain's terrifying forces reflect aspects of its Darklord, that villain becomes a terrifying culmination of the evil in the land and the personification of the domain's terrors.

entries.name: Familiar Ground
entries: Every Domain of Dread takes inspiration from stories within its related genres. Embrace this. Including tropes from your favorite horror stories reinforces expectations about the domain and provides a shorthand to help players understand a location. The Domains of Dread are intentionally filled with gothic castles, haunted manors, and beleaguered villages to hint at the types of characters and adventures you'll find within. Don't worry about recreating familiar stories, as the unpredictable actions of the players' characters will create a unique adventure.

entries.name: Twisted Tropes
entries: Wildly deviating from tropes can create unique locations, but doing so requires more explanation to help players understand the setting. Consider instead featuring familiar horror settings with a twist to make them more appropriate to D&D or to enhance their terror. For example, characters escaping an alien laboratory in {@area Bluetspur|2a5|x} might be stalked by an elusive {@creature displacer beast|XMM}, or the ghosts haunting a house in {@book Mordent|RHW|2} might be desperately trying to keep something worse sealed in the basement. Minor twists help setting elements feel distinct and work better in your game.

entries.name: Isolation
entries: Isolation can take multiple forms in a domain.

entries.name: Character Isolation
entries: In the Domains of Dread, the characters are often the land's only opposition to a domain's evil and their own only hope for survival. While characters might find allies or sanctuaries in a domain, the characters must look to themselves for solutions. Escape is rarely an option.

entries.name: Physical Isolation
entries: Each domain is a complete setting that the Mists might isolate from all other lands. Locations within a domain are often isolated by dark, monster-haunted wildernesses. Even settlements and sanctuaries might have their own grim secrets.

entries.title: ... might discover nightmares worse than they escaped

entries.name: The Locals
entries: The inhabitants of domains endure or ignore the horrors around them. Whether these individuals are oppressed people or soulless pawns of the Dark Powers is up to you, but regardless, they don't effectively resist the horror within their homes. Characters should feel like they're among the only ones who recognize a domain's evil.

entries.name: Incongruous Behavior
entries: Consider treating a domain's inhabitants as part of the strangeness of the setting. Such NPCs might willfully ignore proof of the domain's horrors or passively behave in ways that reinforce a Darklord's will. In more surreal cases, NPCs might forget the characters or even reappear after dying.

entries.name: Nightmare Nature
entries: To any question regarding the natural world within a domain, the scariest answer is likely true. Use details of a domain to create a beat of small, unsettling moments to support the percussion of unignorable terrors in your adventures. If you highlight the strange nature of a domain often, the land feels surreal and haunted. If you do so more rarely, the domain feels more realistic. You might even change the pace of how often you reinforce the strange nature of a domain, perhaps doing so more the farther characters are from civilization or safety.
For examples of frightful details you might highlight in a domain, roll on or choose a result from the Dread Details table.

entries.caption: Dread Details
colLabels: In a Quiet Moment, a Character Witnesses...
rows: Bestial eyes glaring from the shadows.
Clouds briefly take on frightful shapes.
A door unlatch and open on its own.
A farm animal or pet curse under its breath.
A shadow or reflection move on its own.
A spectral orb drift through a wall or door.
A tree branch move like a grasping claw.

entries: You can customize these suggestions to reinforce the themes of a domain or its Darklord. For example, in {@book Falkovnia|RHW|2}, a statue might momentarily resemble a rotting zombie, while in {@book Har'Akir|RHW|2}, Darklord Ankhtepot's visage might appear in a sandstorm. Strive to make such elements feel like moments of strangeness—harmless oddities that a character can dismiss as their eyes deceiving them, rather than suggestions of an immediate threat.

entries.name: Using Darklords
entries: Second only to the characters, Darklords are the most important inhabitants of the Domains of Dread. This section provides details and options for using Darklords in horror campaigns.

entries.name: Darklords in Your Adventures
entries: Most Darklords are important, recurring villains. Take time to understand these characters' origins and develop details that you can consistently express as characters repeatedly encounter them, their agents, and evidence of their deeds. The {@book Dungeon Master's Guide|XDMG} provides details on developing villains as well as an {@book NPC Tracker sheet|XDMG|10|NPC Tracker}. Use the guidance there and in the following sections to develop Darklords into infamous villains your players will be excited to learn about and oppose.

entries.name: Roleplaying a Darklord
entries: Most Darklords are irredeemably evil villains, obsessed with their own drives and desires. Many delude themselves into believing they're heroes or beings beyond morality. When preparing to roleplay a Darklord, review the Darklord's origin story and consider what traits are core to the Darklord. Aim to roleplay the Darklord in a way that makes sense with the villain's origins and that makes you excited to play them as a recurring character.
Then consider the following questions:

items: What do you like most about the Darklord and want to highlight?
What are the Darklord's three most positive traits?
What are the Darklord's three most negative traits?
Why do others fear the Darklord?
What makes the Darklord effective in their role?
Does the Darklord think they're a hero or better than others? Why?
Does the Darklord always lie or never lie?
How is the Darklord a reflection of one or more of the adventurers?
What does the Darklord want more than anything?
When would the Darklord be gracious or heroic?

entries: Record your answers to these questions, then create a short list of characteristics you want to convey to the players over encounters with the Darklord.

entries.name: Darklord Goals
entries: In the adventure {@adventure Curse of Strahd|CoS}, the Darklord's goals are stated as follows:

items: Transform and possess a victim
Torment and slay a rival
Test or toy with the characters

entries: These goals guide Strahd's interactions with the characters through an entire campaign, allowing for the villain's consistent representation.

Recurring Darklords (or other prominent villains) should have similar, simple-to-understand goals. While you might know the finer details of a Darklord's motivations, players engage with villains only a limited number of times—likely with significant amounts of time between interactions. Choose one to three objectives for a Darklord that you can reinforce during character interactions or that you can hint at with other evidence. This helps the players understand what the Darklord wants and how to act against them.
Use the goals presented above and on the list below to inspire your Darklord's objectives:

items: Change society
Claim a new role
Continue a tradition
Correct a tragic event
Defend the status quo
Enforce order
Escape their prison
Find a missing person
Maintain a lie
Prevent the inevitable
Recover a lost treasure
Relieve their boredom
Seek a revelation
Suppress a secret

entries.name: Darklord's Tale
entries: A Darklord's villainy informs their goals and the torments they suffer. As characters adventure in a Darklord's domain, they learn about the Darklord, hear reports of the Darklord's infamy, and eventually discover secrets to frustrate or undermine the villain. Consider these ways of revealing details about a Darklord:

items.name: Rumors and Legends
items.entry: Domain inhabitants typically know of a Darklord or the results of the villain's evil. They can provide consistent reports of the Darklord's depredations, what little they know of the villain's origins, and examples of how their lives are affected by the Darklord's evil.

items.name: Evidence
items.entry: Show the characters examples of the Darklord's evil, the suffering of the villain's victims, the crimes of the Darklord's servants, and how the domain is scarred by the Darklord's wickedness.

items.name: Lies
items.entry: The Darklord or their servants provide flattering but false details of the villain's origins, personality, or objectives. Within such lies are truths commoners don't know.

items.name: Primary Sources
items.entry: The truth of a Darklord's past might come from the villain's family, longtime servants or rivals, or historic records—such as journals, correspondence, or neglected artwork. Magical sources, such as mirrors that reflect the past or methods to enter the Darklord's memories, might also reveal secrets. Such truths might be hidden, but the Dark Powers ensure they survive to torment the Darklord.

entries.title: The Tome of Strahd recounts the tale of Strahd von Zarovich. Other Darklords might keep similar records

entries.name: Darklord's Menace
entries: Not every Darklord is a threat in combat to every party, but a Darklord's menace isn't rooted in their stat block. Rather, it's built over adventures where evidence of the Darklord's wickedness is established and reinforced. Consider the following questions to develop a Darklord's reputation, adding them as features of adventures:

items.name: Demonstrations
items.entry: When the characters offend the Darklord, how does the villain express displeasure? Do they take it out on the domain's inhabitants? Do they dub the characters criminals or heretics and execute innocent people who helped the characters?

items.name: Law and Superstition
items.entry: What do the domain's inhabitants do or refuse to do out of fear of the Darklord or the situation the Darklord has created? Have these habits become local traditions?

items.name: Servants
items.entry: What serves the Darklord? Are they soldiers and servants? Are they monsters or strangely behaving animals? Is it the weather or environment itself? How do such servants make it clear they're extensions of the Darklord's will?

items.name: Signature Evidence
items.entry: How does the Darklord prey or experiment on the domain's inhabitants and what evidence does that leave? If the Darklord executes or sacrifices foes, what is their signature form of slaughter?

items.name: Strangers
items.entry: Do domain inhabitants believe strangers are agents of the Darklord or likely to attract the Darklord's attention? Do they avoid strangers or spy on them for the Darklord?

items.name: Titles
items.entry: What is the Darklord's title? Is it a royal or religious title, or is it an epithet the domain's inhabitants call them?

entries.name: Meeting a Darklord
entries: Your players' first encounter with a Darklord should be memorable. Prior to this encounter, the characters might have heard rumors of the Darklord and witnessed their depravity. Reinforce this by planning how the characters will meet the Darklord and what the villain will be doing. Consider whether the Darklord is...

items: Attempting to hire the characters.
Commanding the Darklord's servants.
Committing a signature crime.
Engaged in an experiment.
Having a decadent or horrible meal.
Impatient with the characters' interruption.
Pretending to be heroic.
Trying to lure a victim.

entries: Pay attention to the Darklord's first words to the characters. {@book Chapter 2|RHW|2} presents greetings along with many Darklords' portraits. Take inspiration from these greetings, and have the Darklord's first words reflect their self-obsessed personalities and how they perceive strangers.

In future meetings, the Darklord should behave consistently. You might develop a signature way of speaking, turns of phrase, or an in-character voice for a Darklord, but keep these simple so the villain doesn't feel like a caricature or comedic figure.

entries.name: Darklord Allies
entries: Determine who serves the Darklord. A Darklord's servants should feel like facets of the Darklord's nature or goals. Characters should overcome these foes during adventures wherein they learn more about the Darklord. Defeating lesser villains gives characters a sense of progress while maintaining a Darklord's menace. Make a Darklord's allies exciting, but don't lose sight of the Darklord as the most important villain.
When developing allies for a Darklord, consider the following types of allies:

items.name: Advisers
items.entry: Advisers grant the villain insights or control they wouldn't otherwise possess.

items.name: Copies
items.entry: Some servants are lesser versions of the Darklord. They do the same things as the Darklord to a lesser extent, or they're a weaker version of the same creature as the Darklord.

items.name: Creations
items.entry: A Darklord might be served by their creations, such as Construct or Undead servants. A relic or ritual might maintain the Darklord's control of such creatures.

items.name: Devotees
items.entry: Devotees of a Darklord follow them without question. They might be whole organizations of soldiers or worshippers.

items.name: Grudging Allies
items.entry: Some servants obey because they must. They might serve the Darklord to keep loved ones safe or out of a sense of duty.

items.name: Incidental Allies
items.entry: Some allies don't directly serve the Darklord, but they and the Darklord both gain benefits from their partnership. A monster the Darklord keeps fed with sacrifices would be such an ally.

items.name: Majordomo
items.entry: A majordomo is likely the Darklord's most powerful servant—the one who knows the most about them and, potentially, the one most likely to betray the Darklord.

items.name: Mini Darklords
items.entry: Some servants control their own portions of a domain and embody the Darklord's will in that region.

entries: Individual allies can occupy multiple roles.

entries.title: The Holy Symbol of Ravenkind is one example of a relic used to defeat a Darklord

entries.name: Defeating a Darklord
entries: Slaying a villain isn't in itself a heroic goal, but the death of a villain generally means an end to its evil and its ability to affect others. Such isn't true of Darklords.

Typically, when a Darklord is defeated in combat, their domain doesn't change, and fear of the Darklord doesn't fade. Moreover, the Darklord returns after a short time. While a Darklord is sidelined, their domain-affecting powers end, their servants go without orders, and the domains' borders open. Characters might enter the Mists and find their threats reduced, but this doesn't mean the Mists allow travel to other lands.

Thwarting a Darklord and escaping their clutches ultimately means delivering the villain a dramatic defeat—one that sends them into such a state of rage, desolation, or uncertainty that their grip on their domain is undermined. Such a defeat might involve a climactic battle following a series of heroic accomplishments. If you plan a climactic confrontation with a Darklord—after which the characters escape the Darklord's domain—determine how the characters must subvert the villain. These requirements should develop from the Darklord's origins and as counters to the goals you've developed for the villain (see "{@area Darklord Goals|2dd|x}" earlier in this chapter). Take inspiration for additional goals for the characters from the suggestions here:

items.name: Destruction
items.entry: Destroy what the Darklord draws their power from, what they've been working to create, or their sanctuary.

items.name: Escape
items.entry: Help a victim or captive of the Darklord escape the domain, or ensure they can't be part of the Darklord's plans.

items.name: Isolation
items.entry: Disrupt those who empower the Darklord—like their soldiers, cultists, creations, or other allies—forcing the Darklord to spend years to make more.

items.name: Relic
items.entry: Recover a symbol, icon, or weapon that is anathema to the Darklord.

items.name: Rivals
items.entry: Empower foes of the Darklord, resurrect storied heroes, restore bastions of opposition, or reawaken sites of good magic within the domain.

items.name: Truth
items.entry: Reveal the Darklord as a fraud or pretender to their position.

entries: Characters should achieve these goals over the course of multiple adventures, culminating in a climactic encounter where the Darklord discovers the full extent of the characters' successes.

entries.name: Unexpected Ends
entries: It's fine if characters defeat a Darklord in combat before you intend. A Darklord loses control only after extraordinary events during an adventure's climax. Until then, any defeat is an inconvenience or was part of a Darklord's plans—in any case, it's an opportunity for adventures. Don't rob characters of the accomplishment of a victory, but don't let them think they've won for long. Use the suggestions below to reinforce a Darklord's threat even after an unexpected defeat.

entries.name: Familiar Death
entries: This isn't Darklord's first death. As they die, a Darklord might be amused, blasé, cocky, or fascinated, but not angry or fearful. As it departs, a Darklord might congratulate the characters or narrate their own end. Before dying, the Darklord should make it clear that the characters now have the Darklord's full attention.

entries.name: No Remains
entries: Darklords don't usually leave corpses. When a Darklord is defeated, the Mists claim their body, or the corpse dissolves in an unsettling or thematically appropriate effect.

entries.name: Deadly in Defeat
entries: With the Darklord's defeat, what contingencies take effect? Perhaps the Darklord left a letter or a gift to be delivered on the event of their death. Maybe their servants have orders about what to do on the event of the Darklord's disappearance—like commencing an attack or making a sacrifice. Perhaps threats the Darklord was restraining—such as hidden monsters or volatile servants—run wild, making the domain's inhabitants desire the Darklord's return. In any case, use a Darklord's defeat to demonstrate that the villain is just the face of a much larger, evil system.

entries.name: The Cat's Away
entries: Use a Darklord's defeat to allow characters to have an adventure they couldn't have had otherwise. Perhaps new allies feel emboldened to share information or the guardians of the Darklord's lair are distracted. Use the Darklord's absence as a chance to reveal more about the villain and provide the characters with the tools they need to defeat the Darklord once and for all.

entries.name: The Darklord's Shadows
entries: The Darklord might be temporarily gone, but their evil isn't. During the Darklord's absence, consider ways the domain might focus more on the characters. Perhaps everyone in the domain whispers about them, wolves are curious about them, zombies flee from them, or the Mists seem to follow them. Cultivate the idea that the Darklord is still watching them or that the characters have violated a taboo.

entries.name: The Darklord's Return
entries: Determine how the Darklord returns and what they do once they're back. Does the Darklord seek revenge against the party? Is the Darklord more curious about—or obsessed with—the characters (see "Darklord Patrons" below)? Does the Darklord invite the characters to their home, devise a trap for them, or seek to involve them in some broader plot? Or was the Darklord's defeat truly inconsequential, with the characters learning of the villain's return only well afterward?

entries.name: Darklord Patrons
entries: Most Darklords have lost track of how long they've been prisoners of their domains. Countless times they've failed to achieve their goals, and countless times the Dark Powers have tempted them with hope and opportunity again. Darklords seek new factors that might finally allow them to achieve their desires. And few factors hold more potential to further Darklords' plots—or simply distract them for a time—than the characters.

Darklords might become fascinated by characters, viewing them as potential allies, protégés, tools, or challenges to overcome. In such cases, the Darklord might patronize or test the characters, sending them on quests throughout the Darklord's domain or into the domains of rival Darklords. In return for the characters' aid, the Darklord might offer the characters escape, secrets, or treasures, or the villain might promise not to harm innocents or the characters' allies.

Darklords might serve as typical quest givers, but they tend to be more possessive of their allies. At your discretion, a Darklord's magic might grant all the characters a Dark Gift from those presented in {@area chapter 1|0a7|x}. Alternatively, the Darklord might grant characters a sinister supernatural gift.

entries.name: Darklord Gifts
entries: Darklords might bestow a supernatural gift on characters they've allied with (see "{@book Supernatural Gifts|XDMG|2|Supernatural Gifts}" in the {@book Dungeon Master's Guide|XDMG} for details). These sinister Blessings might be a symbol of a promise made between the Darklord and the recipient, or they might be a reward for the characters' service. A character must accept a Darklord's Blessing to receive it. The Blessing can be removed by the {@spell Remove Curse|XPHB} spell, or the Darklord might revoke it anytime.
The following Blessings are examples of those a Darklord might bestow upon a character. Customize them to create gifts appropriate to specific Darklords.

entries.name: Mark of Beasts
entries: Supernatural Gift (Blessing)

On granting this Blessing, the Darklord chooses a Beast associated with themself. While you are in the same domain as the Darklord, Beasts of that kind are {@variantrule Friendly [Attitude]|XPHB|Friendly} toward you. Additionally, you can use this Blessing to cast {@spell Speak with Animals|XPHB} to communicate with this kind of Beast only. Once you cast this spell in this way, you can't cast it again until you finish a Long Rest.

entries.name: Mark of the Dread Lord
entries: Supernatural Gift (Blessing)
While in the same domain as the Darklord who gave you this Blessing, you can cast {@spell Fear|XPHB}. Once you cast this spell in this way, you can't cast it again until you finish a Long Rest.
So long as you possess this Blessing, your face is veiled by shadows.

entries.name: Mark of Obsession
entries: Supernatural Gift (Blessing)

Immediately after you take damage from a creature you can see within 10 feet of yourself, you can take a Reaction to force the creature to make a {@dc 14} Wisdom saving throw. On a failed save, the creature has the {@condition Frightened|XPHB} condition until the end of your next turn. Once you use this Blessing, you can't use it again until you finish a Long Rest.

While you have this Blessing, the Darklord who gave it to you can contact you once every 24 hours. This might take the form of a magical message, such as a {@spell Sending|XPHB} spell, or a short letter delivered by mysterious means.

entries.name: Mark of Sacrifice
entries: Supernatural Gift (Blessing)
You can use this Blessing to cast {@spell Protection from Evil and Good|XPHB}. Once you cast this spell in this way, you can't cast it again until you finish a Long Rest.
If this Blessing is removed, the Darklord who gave it to you knows your whereabouts, and you have {@variantrule Disadvantage|XPHB} on Wisdom saving throws for 24 hours.

entries.name: Escaping Ravenloft
entries: The Domains of Dread are as escapable as you please. The Mists might claim characters for a single adventure, then return them home. The characters might be residents of the Lands of the Mists, making escape meaningless. Or escape from Ravenloft might motivate an entire campaign. If you make escape a focus of your campaign, consider the following questions:

items.name: Possibility
items.entry: Is it possible to escape the Domains of Dread against the will of the Dark Powers?

items.name: Experts
items.entry: Who might have knowledge regarding the nature of Ravenloft? What might the characters do to convince them to share their knowledge?

items.name: Methods
items.entry: What methods might allow the characters to escape? Is powerful magic enough, or does the nature of Ravenloft need to be altered? What costs might such methods have?

items.name: Ramifications
items.entry: Will the characters' escape only affect them, or will it have ramifications across the Domains of Dread (or the entire multiverse)?

items.name: Will
items.entry: Do the Dark Powers have a role in the characters' escape? Must characters win these mysterious entities' favor or find ways to defy them?

entries: Roll on or choose a result from Escape Adventure Seeds table to inspire adventure or campaign plots involving escape from the Domains of Dread.

entries.caption: Escape Adventure Seeds
rows: Activate the Rift Spanner, a device fueled by souls stolen by the night hag Styrix.
Free {@area Cthulhu|1a1|x}, allowing it to escape into the multiverse.
Find {@area Azalin Rex|117|x} and discover how he escaped {@book Darkon|RHW|2}.
Journey into the past and prevent {@area Strahd von Zarovich|0d5|x} from becoming the first Darklord.
Sever a Darklord's tie to their domain, freeing the land back into the multiverse.
Use the device called the {@area Apparatus|21b|x} to transport their souls into other bodies.

entries.title: Darklord Ivan Dilisnya invites the objects of his obsessions to share in bizarre diversions at his estate, Degravo

entries.name: Enemies and Allies
entries: This section presents individuals and groups who might appear anywhere in the Lands of the Mists. Such individuals and organizations might create continuity between domains, facilitate travel through the Mists, or provide support to characters. Alternatively, they might serve as opponents capable of confounding characters in multiple domains.

entries.name: Mist Wanderers
entries: Individuals who travel the Mists might ally with characters or share mysteries that lead to adventures elsewhere in the Domains of Dread.

entries.name: Alanik Ray
entries: {@i The Great Detective}

Alanik Ray is a peerless, elf investigator renowned in multiple domains as a master sleuth and solver of confounding mysteries. Alanik resides in Port-a-Lucine in {@book Dementlieu|RHW|2} with his husband, Arthur Sedgwick, but he investigates mysteries and aids sleuths across the Domains of Dread.

entries.name: Arthur Sedgwick
entries: {@i Pragmatic Investigator}

Together with his husband, Alanik Ray, the physician Arthur Sedgwick investigates criminal cases and paranormal mysteries. The practical half of the detective duo, Arthur chronicles their adventures, having published two volumes: {@i The Life of Alanik Ray} and {@i The Casebook of Alanik Ray}.

entries.name: Ez d'Avenir
entries: {@i Fearless Adventurer}

As a former pupil of {@area Dr. Rudolph van Richten|3b5|x}, {@creature Ez d'Avenir|RHW} (detailed in {@area chapter 5|3a3|x}) is no stranger to the dangers haunting the Mists. Since venturing out on her own, Ez has gained a reputation as a skilled monster hunter and foe of evil. She often allies herself with other adventurers to put an end to evils.

entries.name: Firan Zal'honan
entries: {@i Scholar of Sinister Secrets}

The arrogant scholar Firan Zal'honan seeks the secrets underpinning the Domains of Dread. He plots to use his discoveries to escape the Mists into the "true realities" beyond. Firan hires agents to aid in his expeditions or to ferry him through the Mists—which he claims want to disrupt his efforts. Secretly Firan has ties to {@area Azalin Rex|117|x}, the missing Darklord of Darkon, or might be the Darklord in disguise (see {@book chapter 2|RHW|2} for details).

entries.name: Larissa Snowmane
entries: {@i Domain-Traveling Riverboat Captain}

Larissa Snowmane ferries travelers between the Domains of Dread aboard her riverboat, {@i River Dancer}. Larissa protects those traveling with her. In the most dire circumstance, she uses her mysterious magic, the Dance of the Dead, which can control the dead at the risk of slaying the living. Larissa learned her magic in part from Anton Misroi, Darklord of {@area Souragne|2ba|x}. She avoids that swampy domain at all costs.

entries.title: Larissa Snowmane's ship, River Dancer, navigates the waterways of the Domains of Dread

entries.name: Savra Sunstar
entries: {@i Dhampir Vampire Hunter}

Savra Sunstar is a monster hunter and the dhampir daughter of Jander Sunstar, a vampire hero who saved Savra's mother from an early demise. For her part, Savra despises her father and seeks to slay him. She has devoted her life to hunting vampires and allies herself with like-minded adventurers.

entries.name: Weathermay-Foxgrove Twins
entries: {@i Paranormal Investigators}

The twins {@creature Gennifer Weathermay-Foxgrove|RHW|Gennifer} and {@creature Laurie Weathermay-Foxgrove|RHW} (detailed in {@area chapter 5|3c9|x}) are tenacious adventurers who follow their family's tradition of slaying monsters and defending the innocent. They reside in Mordent and have learned much from their surrogate uncle, {@area Rudolph van Richten|3b5|x}.

entries.name: Organizations
entries: These organizations span multiple domains.

entries.name: Church of Ezra

items.name: Goal:
items.entry: Seek mercy from an ambivalent god

items.name: Adventure Themes:
items.entry: Apathetic congregations, superstitions, witch hunting

entries: Pious souls in multiple domains pray to Ezra, an aloof god who personifies the Mists. Ezra's worshippers appear in congregations with varied (and sometimes conflicting) practices. The church serves largely as a formalization of local superstitions, whether in modest rural temples or urban cathedrals. When common folk give voice to their hopes or seek to ward off evil, they often pray to Ezra.

entries.name: The Circle

items.name: Goal:
items.entry: Vanquish evil through acts of self-sacrifice

items.name: Adventure Themes:
items.entry: Bravery, hubris, proof of heroism, quests against evil, tragedy

entries: Bold knights of the Circle quest from the {@book Shadowlands|RHW|2} in search of evil. More often than not, though, they find tragic fates. Even successful knights often venture back to the Shadowlands with evidence of their victories, potentially carrying evil back to their homeland. The knights of the Circle provide bold but reckless assistance to other would-be heroes, endlessly seeking to prove their valor.

entries.name: Keepers of the Feather

items.name: Goal:
items.entry: Discover the paranormal and dispel evil

items.name: Adventure Themes:
items.entry: Paranormal investigations, occult mysteries, mysticism

entries: The founding members of the Keepers of the Feather are {@creature Wereraven|RHW|wereravens} (see {@area chapter 5|3cc|x}) in {@book Barovia|RHW|2} who oppose Strahd and whose reach extends to other domains. These Keepers are cautious, hiding their nature and guiding others so as not to jeopardize themselves or the mystical secrets they've gathered over generations. Many aided by these wereravens aren't ever aware of their assistance.

In other realms, mysteries and talismans of the Keepers of the Feather have fallen into the hands of socialites and spiritualists. In social clubs, curious occultists pursue the mysteries of séances, fortune telling, metaphysical theories, and tales of the macabre. Such dabbling is as likely to cause catastrophes as it is to help those in need. These Keepers identify themselves with amulets that mimic the shape of a Barovian relic known as the {@item Holy Symbol of Ravenkind|CoS}.
Both factions maintain a network of trained ravens that send messages between group members in different domains.

entries.name: Order of the Guardians

items.name: Goal:
items.entry: Destroy or contain cursed items and phenomena

items.name: Adventure Themes:
items.entry: Ending curses, exorcisms, supernatural investigations, vault defense

entries: A network of monastic occultists, the Order of the Guardians hunts and destroys dangerous supernatural objects, cursed items, and stranger anomalies. If the Guardians can't destroy dangerous relics, they contain them within hidden, heavily warded vault-monasteries. The Guardians might share goals with characters trying to prevent supernatural dangers, but they might also consider characters who employ dangerous magic to be threats.

entries.name: Priests of Osybus

items.name: Goal:
items.entry: Release {@area Strahd von Zarovich|0d5|x}

items.name: Adventure Themes:
items.entry: Cult rituals, necromancy, secrets of the Domains of Dread

entries: The {@creature Priest of Osybus|RHW|Priests of Osybus} follow the teachings of a depraved lich and steal souls to transcend death. With their foul immortality, they seek to free Strahd von Zarovich from the Mists, an act they believe will cause the dissolution of the Domains of Dread. This inspires the priests to learn all they can about the deepest mysteries of the Mists. In shadowy cults, they conduct rituals aimed at escaping the Mists. These priests are detailed further in {@area chapter 5|3b0|x}.

entries.name: Ulmist Inquisition

items.name: Goal:
items.entry: Destroy the evil that dwells in wicked hearts

items.name: Adventure Themes:
items.entry: Ends justifying the means, hunting evil, psionic interrogation

entries: The three branches of the Ulmist Inquisition employ psionic powers to stamp out evil, but their zeal and willingness to peer into others' minds cause people to fear the inquisitors as much as the villains they oppose. Cells of the Ulmist Inquisition often ally with the Church of Ezra and oppose the priests of Osybus. Ulmist inquisitors might side with adventurers against evil, but they often test the virtue of their allies' souls. These inquisitors are detailed further in {@area chapter 5|3c0|x}.

entries.name: Vistani

items.name: Goal:
items.entry: Find a way home, preserve age-old traditions, predict and avoid dangers in the Mists

items.name: Adventure Themes:
items.entry: Reliable guides, stories from various lands, travel between domains

entries: Known throughout the Land of the Mists, Vistani (singular: Vistana) have a unique understanding of the Domains of Dread and the hidden paths between them. Following itinerant traditions, many Vistani and their adopted family members travel between domains, learning of distant lands, the many faces of evil, and strange wonders. Vistani never linger in one domain for long, as they endlessly seek a path to their legendary homeland.

entries.name: Horror Atmosphere

entries.title: Light, music, and props all contribute to a frightful atmosphere

entries: Frightful D&D adventures benefit from a game atmosphere that reinforces the horrifying plot. Consider these elements to influence your game's atmosphere, customizing what you can and not fretting about what you can't:

items.name: Location
items.entry: Strive to play in an area with minimal distractions that all players are comfortable in. Reduce distracting stimuli by turning off unnecessary screens and removing nongame materials.

items.name: Lighting
items.entry: Dimming the lights can cause distractions to fade and create an eerie ambiance in your game space. You can also adjust the lighting to reflect a setting's light level, if it's easy for you to do so. However, always ensure the players have enough light to read by.

items.name: Music
items.entry: You can use music to reinforce an adventure's mood. Create playlists in advance, choosing options without lyrics and that aren't recognizably tied to popular media. If you can subtly control volume, slowly raise it when you want attention and lower it when players are talking. Experiment with how volume and tempo influence the mood of a scene.

items.name: Props
items.entry: Props and handouts are useful but shouldn't become chores or distractions. They might be as simple as a whiteboard for notes or a handwritten letter from a Darklord. Ravenloft has a history of featuring props in its adventures, such as the tarokka deck (see {@book appendix A|RHW|6}).

entries.name: Horror Roleplaying
entries: Consider the following methods to develop a sense of dread during your horror adventurers.

entries.name: Describing Horror
entries: Rather than just naming a threat, inspire your players' imaginations by describing the horror indirectly using the following techniques:

items.name: Before It Arrives
items.entry: Foreshadow the horror's approach. How do the environment, nearby animals, the weather, or shadows react to it?

items.name: Approaches
items.entry: What sounds does the horror make as it moves? What parts do characters see before the whole comes into view?

items.name: Up Close
items.entry: What does the horror smell like? How does it breathe? Does the air around it grow cold?

items.name: Attacks
items.entry: How does the horror attack? What does damage from it feel like? When it grabs a character, what does it feel like? Does it have physical weapons, or is it a supernatural force that warps other life?

items.name: Wounded
items.entry: When the horror is wounded, how does it react? What does its blood look like? What is inside it?

entries.name: Horror Pacing
entries: Not everything can be scary, and continuous terror is unsustainable. Before players grow numb to endless tension, create opportunities for them to catch their breath and regain a feeling of calm. The best horror adventures are like roller coasters, ratcheting up suspense and releasing it before building toward the next harrowing moment.

entries.name: Idle Uneasiness
entries: Occasionally slow your adventure's pace to draw out tension. Linger on describing details. Ask players to describe what their characters are thinking or feeling, or ask what the players think is the worst possible thing that could happen right now. Describe feelings of being watched. Let characters respond to these stray thoughts. Sometimes these impressions should just be their nervousness getting to them. But sometimes it's the calm before a threat strikes.

entries.name: Let Players Fill Gaps
entries: Invite players to turn their imaginations against themselves. Ask players to fill in details with questions like these: What does the way it moves remind you of? It emits the worst smell—what is it? Something on the shelf reminds you of a nightmare—what is it? Players' responses don't need to control the narrative; it's up to you whether these responses are true or just the characters' perceptions.

entries.name: Mood Modeling
entries: Model the behaviors you want players to emulate by focusing on the game, letting others speak in turn, heading off encroachment on boundaries, and complimenting exciting choices. Stand or pace during intense moments. Speak faster or anxiously when something scary is happening. Raise your voice during pitched battles, and whisper during tense or conspiratorial moments. Allow yourself to sound uncertain, correct yourself, or pause as if you heard something outside. Ask players, "Are you sure?" By engaging with the suspenseful atmosphere you want to create, you bring it into being at the table.

entries.name: Out of Time
entries: When the time for action comes, make your narration urgent. Speak quickly and in short bursts. Focus less on senses and more on descriptions—a splash of crimson, who knows where it came from? Politely demand players react quickly: "There's no time to debate—what do you do?"

entries.name: Reliability and Trust
entries: Reliability and trust are just as important to your horror adventures as lurid shocks. Frequent betrayals can make players hesitant, so emphasize that certain people and locations are trustworthy through repeated positive encounters.

entries.name: End on Cliffhangers
entries: Leave players excited to play again. When ending a session, implement one of the following to conclude on a cliffhanger: Hint at a new threat, impose a complication on existing plans, increase the stakes, introduce a new mystery, have a foe appear, have the world change, or otherwise shock the characters. Then call for a break before players can react.

entries.name: Haunted Bastions
entries: The rules for {@book Bastions|XDMG|10} in the {@book Dungeon Master's Guide|XDMG} allow characters to establish sanctuaries to rest and accrue resources between adventures, but these redoubts aren't outside the grasp of the Mists.

entries.name: Bastions in the Mists
entries: Bastions in the Domains of Dread work well in campaigns that focus on a single domain or where Darklords aren't likely to seal their domains' borders and cut characters off from their Bastions.

Characters can create Bastions in any domain. The domains of {@book Borca|RHW|2}, {@book Darkon|RHW|2}, {@book Lamordia|RHW|2}, {@book Mordent|RHW|2}, and the {@book Shadowlands|RHW|2} are well suited to hosting Bastions due to their large size or aloof Darklords. Consider having characters inherit one or more existing structures—such as a ruined fortress, secluded tower, or house recently scoured of ghosts. Should characters adopt such a structure, they can refurbish it to their specifications.

How Darklords respond to Bastions is up to you, but don't create plots that are hostile to Bastions. Darklords might ignore a Bastion, be curious about it, or use it as one more way to bind characters within their domains.

entries.title: The Dark Powers might intrude on any sanctuary, even characters' personal Bastions

entries.name: Haunted Bastions
entries: Haunted Bastions are afflicted by unusual supernatural events. These might be unpredictable annoyances at first, but clever characters might turn hauntings to their advantage.
If you use this system, the first special facility characters create in their Bastion is Haunted. A Haunted special facility has the following rules:

items.name: Irreplaceable
items.entry: A Haunted special facility can't be replaced until it is no longer Haunted.

items.name: Repeating
items.entry: While there is at least one Haunted special facility in a Bastion, whenever a character gains a new special facility or replaces a special facility with a different one, roll any die; on an even result, that new facility is Haunted.

entries: When a character issues an order to a Haunted special facility or a {@book Maintain|XDMG|7|Maintain} order to their whole Bastion, roll {@dice 1d6}; on a 1, roll on the Bastion Hauntings table and refer to the "{@area Haunting Descriptions|30f|x}" section to see what happens. A Bastion can experience only one haunting per Bastion turn.

entries.name: Haunting Descriptions
entries: The hauntings from the Bastion Hauntings table are detailed here in alphabetical order.

entries.name: Cursed Hireling
entries: One of your Bastion's hirelings is cursed. As long as the hireling remains cursed, it won't leave the special facility it manages, and any work conducted in that facility takes twice as long to complete.

entries.name: Free-Roaming Spirit
entries: A random special facility in your Bastion becomes Haunted. If all the special facilities in the Bastion are already Haunted, the DM rolls again on the {@book Bastion Hauntings table|RHW|3|Bastion Hauntings}.

entries.name: Friendly Ghost
entries: A benevolent spirit possesses a hireling of the DM's choice. If the special facility where the hireling works produces items or Gold Pieces, it produces twice as much as usual as long as the hireling remains possessed. The spirit departs after 14 days.

entries.name: Hidden Room
entries: The spirits reveal a hidden closet within your Bastion. Add a 5-foot-square space to your Bastion. Something might lie inside, at the DM's discretion.

entries.name: Lightning Storm
entries: Trade orders issued this turn have no effect (including one that triggered this haunting, if applicable). If you have a {@facility Lightning Generator|RHW} special facility, this Bastion Haunting has no effect.

entries.name: Living Dead
entries: A group of mindless undead shamble from the Mists. The {@book Attack|XDMG|7|Attack} Bastion event occurs, but roll {@dice 3d6} for that event rather than {@dice 6d6}.

entries.name: Mist Wanderers
entries: The Mists rise and {@dice 1d4} strangers emerge, seeking shelter. If there is room in your Bastion, they join as hirelings. Otherwise, they linger for {@dice 1d4} days before vanishing into the Mists.

entries.name: Mysterious Priest
entries: A hooded priest appears at the Bastion, attracted by its haunting. Roll any die. One of the following occurs based on whether you rolled an even or an odd number on the die:

items.name: Inquisitor (Even)
items.entry: The priest is a witch hunter. If you have an {@facility Ancient Altar|RHW}, {@book Arcane Study|XDMG|7|Arcane Study}, {@facility Cabinet of Curiosities|RHW}, or {@facility Séance Parlor|RHW} in your Bastion, the priest destroys it and you must replace it with a different special facility. If your Bastion has none of these facilities, the {@book Extraordinary Opportunity|XDMG|7|Extraordinary Opportunity} Bastion event occurs.

items.name: Exorcist (Odd)
items.entry: The priest is an exorcist. If you have a {@book Reliquary|XDMG|7|Reliquary}, {@book Sacristy|XDMG|7|Sacristy}, {@book Sanctuary|XDMG|7|Sanctuary}, or {@book Sanctum|XDMG|7|Sanctum} in your Bastion, the priest offers to remove a haunting from one facility. If you agree, choose one Haunted facility; that facility is no longer Haunted.

entries.name: Ill Luck
entries: Accidents and bad luck plague your Bastion. {@book Harvest|XDMG|7|Harvest} orders issued this turn have no effect (including one that triggered this haunting, if applicable).

entries.name: Poltergeist
entries: Disruptive happenings plague the Bastion. {@book Empower|XDMG|7|Empower} and {@book Research|XDMG|7|Research} orders issued this turn have no effect (including one that triggered this haunting, if applicable).

entries.name: Spatial Warp
entries: The nature of a Bastion special facility changes. Roll {@dice 1d4}. On a 4, replace one of your Bastion's special facilities with another for which you qualify; otherwise, increase the {@book size|XDMG|7|Facility Space} of a Cramped special facility in your Bastion to Roomy or a Roomy one to Vast, at no cost.

entries.name: Spectral Vision
entries: A ghostly vision of a past event plays out. You gain some insight into the spirits that haunt your Bastion or the history of the surrounding land, as though affected by the {@spell Legend Lore|XPHB} spell.

entries.name: Vistani Visitors
entries: Vistani travelers visit your Bastion, drawn by tales of the spirits that inhabit it. In return for a night's shelter, they offer to reveal your fate via a casting of the {@spell Augury|XPHB} spell or through a tarokka reading (see {@area appendix A|3dd|x}). If you turn them away, they depart, but the highest-level special facility in your Bastion that isn't already Haunted becomes Haunted.

entries.name: Special Facilities
entries: Characters can create the following special facilities in addition to those in the {@book Dungeon Master's Guide|XDMG}. These facilities are summarized on the Special Facilities table.

entries.title: Spirits and grim secrets haunt Bastions in the Demiplanes of Dread

data.name: Chapter 4: Creating Domains of Dread

entries.title: A hexblood witch in the domain of Tepest conjures visions of adventurers' fatal fates

entries: Any fear might find terrible life amid the Domains of Dread. Each domain highlights its own distinctive terror or flavor of the macabre. {@book Chapter 2|RHW|2} presents numerous Domains of Dread and their respective Darklords, but you can blend legendary evils, unexpected monstrosities, and grim settings into your own horrifying creations.

This chapter explores how to create a Domain of Dread, starting with defining its Darklord, the villain at the domain's heart. Descriptions of various genres of horror also provide details to guide and inspire your creations.

entries.name: Tarokka and Random Tables
entries: This section provides random tables compatible with the tarokka deck (see {@book appendix A|RHW|6}). Here's how to use the tarokka deck—or playing cards—instead of a {@dice d10} to generate random results:

items.name: 1: Remove the High Deck
items.entry: Remove all high deck cards, leaving only cards numbered one through nine plus the master of the suit.

items.name: 2: Draw a Card
items.entry: Ensure the deck is well shuffled, then draw from the top.

items.name: 3: Consult a Table
items.entry: Reference the card's number to find your result on a table; in the tarokka deck, the master of a suit counts as a ten.

entries: When using the tarokka deck, you can let the card's suit add extra dimension to your results. The four of coins might encourage you to link that aspect of the Darklord to wealth, while the four of swords could provide an interpretation rooted in violence. Appendix A details interpretations of the tarokka deck's cards.

entries.name: Creating a Darklord
entries: Domains are reflections of and prisons for the Darklords they confine. To devise a Domain of Dread, first consider its central antagonist and prisoner: its Darklord. The following sections detail how to develop a Darklord as a focal antagonist in your horror adventures.

entries.name: Building Your Darklord
entries: A Darklord's memories, desires, mistakes, and evil deeds shape a domain and its inhabitants. This chapter presents tables with suggested questions and motifs related to a Darklord's past and personality. Choose or randomly generate prompts to develop your Darklord further, or use the table entries as inspiration.
To build a Darklord, follow these steps:

items.name: Step 1:
items.entry: Choose Genres. What horror genres does your Darklord fit into? Use those genres' principles to help develop your Darklord.

items.name: Step 2:
items.entry: Determine Their Past. Who was the Darklord before they turned to irredeemable evil? What ambitions and flaws drove them to commit heinous acts? How did the Darklord first attract the attention of the Dark Powers? The "{@area Darklord's Past|33c|x}" section provides a multitude of prompts to inspire your Darklord's origin story.

items.name: Step 3:
items.entry: Determine Their Identity. What monstrous form does your Darklord's evil take? Turn to each genre chosen in step 1, then consult that genre's respective "Villain Identities" and "Villain Torments" sections. Feel free to mix and match the tables from different genres to achieve your desired feel.

entries.name: Genres of Horror
entries: Horror stories span a wide variety of genres. One tale might explore the grotesque, warping flesh and bone into perversions of the living; another might be fantastical and philosophical, using restless ghosts to examine the nature of the soul.

Before you begin building a Darklord, consider which genres of horror you'd like to explore. The Genres of Horror table describes the six major genres of horror explored later in this chapter and their common motifs. If you're uncertain where to start, use the table to identify which motifs interest, excite, or inspire fear in you. The sections later in the chapter also detail each genre's core principles, which drive how you develop the Darklord, the domain, and the adventures within.

These genres aren't mutually exclusive. Feel free to blend themes from different genres to create a unique and terrifying story. The end of this chapter briefly describes a handful of additional genres for consideration.

entries.caption: Genres of Horror
colLabels: Characters in Stories of This Genre...
rows: Gore, loss of bodily autonomy, parasitic infestations
Faceless terrors, infinite voids, unblinking eyes
Battle grim evils and wicked magic.
Cryptic legends, masked cults, secluded towns
Decrepit mansions, incorporeal haunts, old ruins
Ancient castles, strong emotions, tarnished splendor

entries.name: Darklord's Past
entries: Every Darklord has committed irredeemable acts that attract the attention of the Dark Powers. Yet Darklords are still individuals who, at one point, had a home, childhood, and family. The following sections each examine a different aspect of the Darklord's life prior to imprisonment.

entries.name: Past Life
entries: What circumstances and choices pushed the Darklord down their twisted path? Choose or randomly generate at least three questions from the Past Life table to answer. If you generate the same question again, use the question one above or below the entry instead.

entries.caption: Past Life
rows: Does the Darklord feel that they have any hope of redemption? Do they want it?
How did the Darklord react when challenged?
What ideal or philosophy governs the Darklord's greater actions?
What locations and circumstances shaped the Darklord's adolescence?
What pushed the Darklord to continue committing heinous acts?
What person, place, or object does the Darklord desire most?
What was the first depraved act the Darklord committed?
Whose respect did the Darklord crave?

entries.name: Dark Catalyst
entries: Negative emotions can twist one's ideals into something sinister. Choose or randomly generate at least one prompt from the Dark Catalyst table to develop what encouraged the Darklord's corruption.

entries.caption: Dark Catalyst
rows: Caused them to obsess over control.
Fueled an all-consuming fear of loss.
Pushed them to turn to a greater power.
Sowed paranoia, encouraging them to constantly test their allies' loyalty.

entries.name: Wicked Ambitions
entries: All Darklords have a core ambition or goal that drives them to commit evil acts. These unobtainable goals fueled their corruption and motivate the Darklords' conflicts with adventurers in their domains. Choose or randomly generate such a goal from the Wicked Ambitions table.

entries.caption: Wicked Ambitions
rows: Explores ways to forget the past.
Longs for divine or otherworldly power.
Pursues ways to relieve themself of loneliness.
Relentlessly pursues the reincarnation of a loved one.
Searches for an object or artifact.
Seeks vengeance against a person or ideal.
Wants to be a hero—but one who is twisted to the extreme.
Wishes to be beautiful and admired.

entries.name: Intervention of the Dark Powers
entries: Consider how the Darklord attracted the attention of the Dark Powers. On the completion of an act of remarkable depravity, the Mists claim the Darklord—and perhaps their home—imprisoning them in a new Domain of Dread.
