The deed that ultimately causes a villain to be claimed by the Mists needn't be devastating, but it should represent an evil novel enough for the Dark Powers to take note. Use the Darklords' tales from {@book chapter 2|RHW|2} to inspire how a Darklord might be claimed by the Mists.

entries.name: Birth of a Darklord
entries: Now trapped in a domain tailor-made to be their prison, the Darklord is immortal, and their greatest desire is just out of reach. At this point, you should have an idea of who the Darklord is, what they did, what form their evil takes, and how they came to the attention of the Dark Powers.
Now consider how the Darklord's evil manifests in their domain and how it brings them into conflict with the adventurers and the domain's inhabitants.

entries.name: Monstrous Identities
entries: Some Darklords undergo harrowing transformations on their imprisonment, as the Dark Powers warp those Darklords' forms to reflect their evil. Alternatively, the Darklord might have been a monster to begin with, or perhaps they gained their form and powers through their deeds or a curse.

Consider how your Darklord is viewed by inhabitants of their domain. Reference the genres of horror that your Darklord fits into (later in {@area this chapter|34d|x}), and use a genre's "Villain Identities" and "Villain Torments" sections to shape the Darklord's monstrousness. The "Villain Identities" sections provide options for the Darklord's primary identity or form while within their domain, while the "Villain Torments" sections provide torturous twists that the Dark Powers might employ to torment the Darklord and reflect their evil.

entries.name: Finishing Touches
entries: The story of a Darklord entwines with those of the adventurers and the domain itself. As you finish fleshing out your Darklord's details, consider the following options to further connect the Darklord to their domain and the adventures within it.

entries.name: Endless Torment
entries: In every domain, its Darklord's greatest desire remains in view but ever out of reach. How does your Darklord's torment remain central to and unignorable in the story?

entries.name: Misty Borders
entries: The {@area Mists of Ravenloft|006|x} surround each Domain of Dread. The Dark Powers afford Darklords some control over the Mists bordering their domains. Does your Darklord allow the Mists to function undisturbed, or do the Mists also reflect the Darklord's nature, perpetuating the cycle of torment?

entries.name: Supporting Cast
entries: Consider what NPC types support the Darklord, exacerbate their threat, or oppose them. How do the inhabitants of the domain play into the Dark Powers' plans, taking the Darklord's situation from bad to worse?

entries.name: Creating a Domain

entries.title: Domains of Dread might be any size or shape and can draw inspiration from any horror genre

entries: Once you've developed a terrifying Darklord, start shaping that Darklord's unique Domain of Dread. Everything in this realm is inspired by the Darklord's torment or personalized to it.
This section provides guidelines to help you create a suitably terrifying and tragic Domain of Dread.

entries.name: Building a Domain
entries: Similar to building a Darklord, you can choose or randomly generate prompts from the tables below to flesh out the domain's details.
To build a Domain of Dread, follow these steps:

items.name: Step 1:
items.entry: Choose Genres. Most Domains of Dread are inspired by the same horror genres that shape their imprisoned Darklord, but feel free to mix in new genre elements.

items.name: Step 2:
items.entry: Develop Details. What are the distinctive elements of the domain? How do these elements reflect and twist the Darklord's past, furthering the Darklord's torment? The "Dread Details" section contains several tables with prompts to tailor your domain to its central prisoner.

items.name: Step 3:
items.entry: Create Adventures. What adventures will the characters have in this domain? Turn to the genres chosen in step 1, and consult their "Adventure Hooks" and "Adventure Settings" sections to inspire your adventures.

items.name: Step 4:
items.entry: Choose Monsters. The genres later in {@area this chapter|34d|x} each include a table suggesting a plethora of monsters that suit the genre's sensibilities. Consult those monster tables, and choose which terrifying creatures will haunt the characters in the domain. If none of the suggested monsters fit your ideas, you can reskin or alter the suggested monsters or choose ones that do.

entries.name: Dread Details
entries: The domain's buildings, natural elements, landmarks, and creatures reflect the Darklord's vile past. This section provides prompts for developing elements of your domain and connecting those specifics to the imprisoned Darklord.

Don't get bogged down with the particulars of a working society. Things like weather, ecosystems, and agriculture needn't follow logic in a domain. Mundane details can fade into the background as your adventures focus on the domain's threats.

entries.name: Domain Elements
entries: Above all else, a domain is a torturous prison for its Darklord. Visceral sights, sounds, and smells within the domain should hint at the Darklord's past villainy or serve as a means for the Dark Powers to torment the domain's central prisoner.

Choose or randomly generate at least three questions from the Domain Elements table to answer. If you generate the same question again, use the question one above or below the entry instead. Don't be afraid to combine details in unexpected ways—the more unnatural or off-putting, the better.

entries.caption: Domain Elements
rows: What sounds, smells, colors, or symbols pervade the domain?
Where do the Mists appear besides the domain's borders? What shapes and sounds haunt the Mists in and near the domain?
What is visible in the sky? Is there a day-and-night cycle? What's the weather like?
What is the domain culture's general attitude toward the Darklord?
What does the domain culture fear or consider taboo?
What does the domain culture celebrate?
What is scarce in the domain, and how do the inhabitants compensate for this?
What do buildings in the domain look like? Are they new or old?
What biome is the domain's environment (e.g., arctic, desert, forest)?
What justifications do the inhabitants use, if any, to pretend nothing is wrong in the domain?

entries.name: Darklord's Shadows
entries: A domain's features are constant, painful, and symbolic reminders of the Darklord's wicked past. Choose or randomly generate at least one prompt from the Darklord's Shadows table to develop how the domain reflects the Darklord's history. Consider connecting the results to your answers to the questions in "Domain Elements."

entries.caption: Darklord's Shadows
rows: Someone or something the Darklord loved—and lost.
Esoteric knowledge the Darklord sought but couldn't obtain.
A tradition sacred to the Darklord's home.
An act of extreme violence the Darklord committed.
Evidence of the Darklord's vilest act.

entries.name: Unleash Your Horror
entries: At this point, you should have a wealth of information about your Darklord and their domain. Review your notes, connect elements, establish recurring themes, and consider ways to foreshadow circumstances that adventures might focus on. You don't need to have answers for every question now; you can define many details during adventures or by focusing on elements your players gravitate toward. For examples of fully detailed Darklords and domains, see {@book chapter 2|RHW|2}.

entries.name: Genres of Horror
entries: The following sections provide overviews of several genres of horror. Use these to inspire adventures, Darklords, and Domains of Dread of your own creation or to further detail the domains presented in {@book chapter 2|RHW|2}. Mix and match elements from different genres to create your own uniquely terrifying combinations.

entries.name: Body Horror
entries: Body horror examines the fear of our failing anatomies. Tales in this genre explore the unpredictability of flesh—that robust health might be an illusion and that we might be incubating what will devour us. Examples include {@i Alien} and {@i The Fly}.

entries.name: Body Horror Principles
entries: Consider the following principles when creating Darklords and domains with body horror themes:

items.name: Depth of Transformation
items.entry: Physical transformations are common and might affect more than bodies. Objects, architecture, and the natural world might be also anthropomorphized.

items.name: Flesh Aberrations
items.entry: Characters suffer aberrant or excessive growth, whether of organs, discordant appendages, or unnatural materials.

items.name: Gore Is a Byproduct
items.entry: Though gore might take center stage in body horror stories, it's often a side effect of stories about fear of change, difference, mortality, physical harm, or disease.

items.name: Gore Isn't Required
items.entry: Body horror doesn't need to be messy. Swapping bodies, turning to stone, or gaining one's ideal form might feature in this genre.

items.name: Real Disabilities Aren't Horrifying
items.entry: Body horror has a history of portraying disability as monstrous. Avoid this trope.

entries.name: Body Horror Villains
entries: A physical transformation doesn't make someone a villain. Villains in body horror adventures cultivate evil in tandem with the aberrant transformations afflicting their form and surroundings.

entries.name: Villain Identities
entries: Villains of this genre are often tragic figures, whether they're cultists enduring forced metamorphosis or scientists trying to extend their lives. Choose from or roll on the Body Horror Villains table to inspire villains appropriate to this genre.

entries.caption: Body Horror Villains
rows: An abandoned homunculus made in the image of its creator's child.
A scientist who, hoping to keep their spouse alive, grafts their spouse to their own body.
A fungal mass that grows more intelligent with every sapient life it engulfs.
A guardian angel possessed by the vile blood of the demons it has slain.
A dying ruler obsessed with creating a new body so they can continue their reign.
A cancer possessed by the mind of a dead mage that seeks to regrow their body.
A monarch who feeds their cannibal children, no matter the cost.
The priests of a forgotten god, who try to raise their deity from the faithful's flesh.
A house that remembers its tenants and will do anything to regain them.
A grieving mortician who sculpts every face they encounter into that of their lost love.

entries.name: Villain Torments
entries: Villains in this genre simultaneously draw power from and are tormented by supernatural and physiological aberrance. Choose from or roll on the Body Horror Torments table to determine what abhorrent affliction plagues the villain.

entries.caption: Body Horror Torments
rows: Possesses a bestial mouth in their torso.
Is pockmarked with eyes that never close.
Grows tooth-filled cysts on their limbs.
Is plagued by an ever-growing fungal mass sprouting from their extremities.
Has parasites writhing beneath their skin.
Can dissolve their physical form at will.
Is gravid with bulbous egg sacs.
Can't control their transformation into a beast and back.

entries.name: Body Horror Adventures
entries: Adventures in body horror realms often become tests of endurance, whether characters are attempting to hold out until rescue arrives or endeavoring to mount a rescue themselves. Alternatively, adventures might have an investigative feel, requiring the heroes to uncover the dark secret that lies beneath the mundane.

entries.name: Adventure Hooks
entries: Body horror plots often explore the unpredictability of life and discordance between mind and body. Choose from or roll on the Body Horror Plots table to inspire your body horror adventures.

entries.caption: Body Horror Plots
rows: A malevolent curse is transforming the village's children into misshapen statues.
Guests of a popular local dinner party keep succumbing to autophagia.
Corpses of beasts—mutated and halfway human—are left at the forest's edge.
Fiends have infiltrated a community by wearing the corpses of the deceased.
Fleshy catacombs open beneath a town.
A neighborhood is filled with derelict houses composed entirely of mimics.
The corpse of a monstrous leviathan has washed ashore, entrails still moving.
A virulent infestation uses the townsfolk as incubators for giant insects.
Something is stealing the tongues of the city guards.

entries.name: Adventure Settings
entries: Body horror can occur anywhere, from mundane settings to unsettling locales. Choose from or roll on the Body Horror Settings table to inspire locations for your adventures.

entries.caption: Body Horror Settings
rows: A vine-covered, ostensibly abandoned prison or asylum.
The cavernous gut of a dead behemoth.
A sprawling university, older than the town surrounding it.
A city of red muscle, with bleeding eyes embedded in hair-strangled buildings.
A forest of burnt pines draped in gore.
A derelict laboratory with old experiments floating in containment tubes.
A slaughterhouse, larger than it appears on the outside and full of victims.
An old church sitting astride a warren of ancient tunnels.
A vibrant garden sprouting from a mound of putrid corpses.

entries.name: Body Horror Monsters
entries: Any monster might become the focus of a body horror plot. Know what your players anticipate so you can distort those expectations. A poised elf revealed to be a husk puppeted by sapient worms can prove as frightening as any eldritch horror. The Body Horror Monsters table presents monsters particularly appropriate to such adventures. Unless otherwise noted, creatures in this table appear in the {@book Monster Manual|XMM}.

entries.caption: Body Horror Monsters
rows: {@creature Blue Slaad|XMM}, {@creature Bodytaker Plant|RHW},* {@creature Necrichor|RHW},* {@creature Violet Fungus Necrohulk|XMM}

entries.name: Cosmic Horror
entries: Cosmic horror revolves around the fear of personal insignificance. The genre, inspired by stories of the Cthulhu Mythos, is predicated on the idea of entities so vast and so genuinely beyond our comprehension that we can't fathom their simplest motivations. To see them is to become lost in their magnitude and the evidence that we have never and could never matter to the cosmos at large. Examples include {@i Annihilation} and {@i Stalker}.

entries.name: Cosmic Horror Principles
entries: Consider the following principles when creating Darklords and domains with cosmic horror themes:

items.name: Fear the Unknown
items.entry: Be vague. Cosmic horror emerges through imagination and the indescribable, not through details.

items.name: Iconography
items.entry: Cults, forbidden books, and strange symbols form the cornerstones of cosmic horror.

items.name: Inscrutable Forces
items.entry: Cosmic horror is about alien forces driven by motivations humans can't understand. There is no good or evil, no law or chaos.

items.name: Marginalization Isn't Horror
items.entry: The genre has a history of framing marginalized demographics as monstrous and stigmatizing mental illness. Avoid those tropes.

items.name: Remove the Familiar
items.entry: Cosmic horror makes characters feel gradually unmoored from reality.

entries.name: Cosmic Horror Villains
entries: Despite the genre's grand magnitude, villains of cosmic horror are still often individual beings. Godlike terrors and inscrutable forces might pull the strings with their unknowable goals, but the antagonists the characters face are concrete, fallible people.

entries.name: Villain Identities
entries: Cosmic horror villains often perpetrate horrors as they strive for outcomes they can't articulate. Choose from or roll on the Cosmic Horror Villains table to inspire villains appropriate to this genre.

entries.caption: Cosmic Horror Villains
rows: A smiling minstrel whose music drives listeners to murder.
A priest obsessed with creating a shelter safe from the coming apocalypse.
A mayor who will do anything to ensure citizens finish a sacred transformation.
An astronomer broken and enraptured by what they saw in the stars.
A scientist striving to make their body the perfect host for an ageless being.
A coroner who believes the bodies they autopsy contain secret messages.
The head librarian of an ancient sect, who seeks secrets hidden within their peers.
A spoiled noble who intends to raise a cult to feed to residents of an alien dimension.
A researcher knowingly incubating a powerful entity within themself.
A hierophant seeking a doorway to an alien realm to bring about a new world.

entries.name: Villain Torments
entries: Villains in this genre are endowed with unnatural powers stemming from their studies or granted by alien forces. Choose from or roll on the Cosmic Horror Torments table to determine what alien or psychological torment plagues the villain.

entries.caption: Cosmic Horror Torments
rows: Is haunted by otherworldly masters that whisper from reflective surfaces.
Is transforming into a long-extinct being.
Screams messages from the far-flung future.
Absorbs the words out of books.
Drains any object they focus on of all but one portentous color.
Finds their body warped to match that of the last person they talked with.
Incubates an alien symbiote in their brain.
Sleepwalks, drawing the same symbol on every surface they come across.
Obsesses over music, shifting their form to embody whatever song they hear.

entries.name: Cosmic Horror Adventures
entries: Adventures in cosmic horror realms are bleak, desperate affairs, where the best one can hope for is to survive relatively intact. There is no stopping the ultimate evil, but characters might aspire to seal it away temporarily.

entries.name: Adventure Hooks
entries: Cosmic horror adventures often deal with how alien forces might alter us, perverting our expectations and separating us from what makes us human. Choose from or roll on the Cosmic Horror Plots table to inspire your cosmic horror adventures.

entries.caption: Cosmic Horror Plots
rows: A child has gone missing in the impossibly vast space beneath their bed.
Something drags a coastal town's inhabitants out in the night, garbs them in silver, and leads them to the surf to drown.
A wandering minstrel plays music that robs entire cities of sleep.
Local bakers have started making pies filled with a delicious, luminescent, alien, blue goo.
A sapient, unholy tome will do anything to reach a group of cultists.
The inheritors of an ancient sect are transforming into astral horrors.
Animals in the local forest have started going to a specific charred grove to die.
Colossal statues surrounding the city move only during dusk and dawn.
The characters must survive an evening in a sinking ancestral home.
A new wing in the academy's library has appeared, filled with blank books.

entries.name: Adventure Settings
entries: Cosmic horror frequently takes place in settings that imply access to hidden knowledge or in unnatural, logic-defying dimensions far removed from reality. Choose from or roll on Cosmic Horror Settings table to inspire locations for your adventures.

entries.caption: Cosmic Horror Settings
rows: A lavish manor devoid of people—save for staff who swear the lord is busy.
A narrow chapel in the woods, whose bell rings ceaselessly.
A hidden ward of a decrepit university.
A dimension of featureless white, broken up only by staring eyes.
The ninth basement beneath an otherwise normal family home.
A small inn in the mountains that smells perpetually of brine.
An ambulatory forest riddled with carvings of unblinking eyes.
A sewage system that predates its city.
A coastline slowly being consumed by an obsidian sea.
A decrepit lighthouse whose light flickers on at strange intervals.

entries.name: Cosmic Horror Monsters
entries: Monsters in cosmic horror adventures are often strange beings that enact bizarre changes on their victims. The Cosmic Horror Monsters table presents monsters particularly appropriate to such adventures. Unless otherwise noted, creatures in this table appear in the {@book Monster Manual|XMM}.

entries.caption: Cosmic Horror Monsters
rows: {@creature Brain in a Jar|RHW},* {@creature Grell|XMM}, {@creature Yuan-ti Malison (Type 1)|XMM}

entries.name: Dark Fantasy
entries: The dark fantasy genre features fantasy worlds where grim themes, nihilistic plots, or horrifying elements inform their stories. Any tale featuring both supernatural elements and horror themes might constitute dark fantasy, whether it's a fantasy story steeped in dread or an otherwise realistic world that features a supernatural terror. Examples include {@i The Dark Tower} and {@i Pan's Labyrinth}.

entries.name: Dark Fantasy Principles
entries: Consider the following principles when creating Darklords and domains with dark fantasy themes:

items.name: Evil Sometimes Triumphs
items.entry: Good doesn't always win. Evil individuals with great power and unopposed schemes might prosper.

items.name: Low Magic
items.entry: Magic and magic items might be rarer or require a bargain or sacrifice to use.

items.name: Morally Gray
items.entry: The lines between good and evil are blurred. Choices involve deciding on the least bad outcome available.

items.name: Nefarious Deeds
items.entry: Corruption and suspicion flourish among organizations and individuals.

items.name: Reluctant Heroes
items.entry: Antiheroes are common—characters tainted by the world's evil or those who refuse to be considered heroes.

entries.name: Dark Fantasy Villains
entries: Sinister individuals leave their marks on dark fantasy domains, whether with oppressive architecture, unavoidable propaganda, or scars on an exploited environment.

entries.name: Villain Identities
entries: Villains occupy places of influence in dark fantasy domains. Choose from or roll on the Dark Fantasy Villains table to inspire villains appropriate to this genre.

entries.caption: Dark Fantasy Villains
rows: A machine that believes it's a resurrected tyrant and seeks to rebuild its empire.
The leader of a subterranean people who plots to blot out the sun's searing light.
A high priest intent on shifting an entire nation into their god's realm.
A desperate general who unleashes otherworldly war machines.
A massive treant who has allied with raiders and seeks to fell every forest.
A sage who solves an endless war by preventing anyone from dying.
An eternal royal whose immortality relies on the blood of angels.
A criminal kingpin who burns opponents into psychoactive spirit dust.
An ancient dragon whose godlike magic drains the domain of life.
A god who killed all their peers and now rules the mortal realm.

entries.name: Villain Torments
entries: In dark fantasy adventures, a villain's power is often rooted in the villain's own suffering. Choose from or roll on the Dark Fantasy Torments table to determine how this power tragically works against the villain.

entries.caption: Dark Fantasy Torments
rows: Uncontrollably damages everything they care about.
Manufactures catastrophes to distract from their own ineptitude.
Is surrounded by power-hungry spies and saboteurs, fueling the villain's paranoia.
Seeks the pleasure of lost glories.
Is surrounded by sycophants whose constant praise makes every success feel hollow.
Is plagued by a cursed family legacy.
Is hounded by rivals and baseless mockery.
Must sacrifice those they love to maintain their facade of power.
Is praised by society for a deed they never truly accomplished.
Elicits excessive fear and reverence, resulting in their isolation.

entries.name: Dark Fantasy Adventures
entries: Adventures in dark fantasy domains involve desperate or reluctant heroes resisting evil. Even in the grimmest settings, desperation and hope fan the flames of resistance.

entries.name: Adventure Hooks
entries: Plots in dark fantasy adventures range from street-level depravity to world-ruining conspiracies. Choose from or roll on the Dark Fantasy Plots table to inspire your dark fantasy adventures.

entries.caption: Dark Fantasy Plots
rows: A beholder-shaped statue has come to life and begun terrorizing the city.
A portal to a demonic realm has opened in the mouth of an innocent acolyte.
A sapient virus is turning the inhabitants of a village into shadows.
Giant spiders have declared war on all bipedal creatures.
A coven plans to use a volcano as a massive cauldron for a catastrophic spell.
The nation's beloved monarch was recently revealed to be a lich.
A malevolent phantom haunts the moon, raising corpses bathed in its light as undead.
A sentient weapon transforms its surroundings into a mockery of the war it last fought in.
A factory distills souls into a powerful wish-granting elixir.
An expanding labyrinth has just swallowed another neighborhood.

entries.name: Adventure Settings
entries: Evil is entrenched in dark fantasy domains, reflected by their grandiose adventure locales. Choose from or roll on the Dark Fantasy Settings table to inspire locations for your adventures.

entries.caption: Dark Fantasy Settings
rows: The floating and fallen ruins of a magical megastructure.
A tower that pierces a magical ley line, draining its power.
A forest where every tree grows from the body of a mummified hero.
An empire covered in the watchful religious symbols of an all-seeing deity.
A fortress with seven locked gates that seal off the underworld.
A country devastated by the fallout of weapons used in an age-old war.
A town threatened by an unstoppable, slowly encroaching infection.
A city adrift on a sea full of primeval predators.

entries.name: Dark Fantasy Monsters
entries: Any monster can find a place in a dark fantasy plot. D&D's most iconic threats—such as dragons and beholders—are particularly appropriate to horror-tinged tales, as are any other supernatural foes. The Dark Fantasy Monsters table presents monsters particularly appropriate to such adventures. Unless otherwise noted, creatures in this table appear in the {@book Monster Manual|XMM}.

entries.caption: Dark Fantasy Monsters
rows: {@creature Displacer Beast|XMM}, {@creature Goblin Hexer|XMM}, {@creature Mist Horror|RHW},* {@creature Waxwork|RHW}*
{@creature Fomorian|XMM}, {@creature Inquisitor of the Mind Fire|RHW},* {@creature Vampire Nightbringer|XMM}

entries.name: Folk Horror
entries: Folk horror adventures involve traditions, beliefs, and perceptions that are passed down through generations and take terrifying twists. For those who ascribe to hidden traditions, sacrifices to strange gods or placations to lurking monsters are everyday events. For outsiders, though, these practices reveal the subjectivity of normalcy and societal truth. Examples include {@i Midsommar and The Wicker Man}.

entries.name: Folk Horror Principles
entries: Consider the following principles when creating Darklords and domains with folk horror themes:

items.name: Isolation Begets Weirdness
items.entry: Strange and potentially dangerous traditions flourish in isolated or private communities.

items.name: Perversion of Community
items.entry: Communities in folk horror stories often are a grim mirror of an aspect of accepted society. They often dramatically use outsiders or purge nonbelievers.

items.name: Relative Truth
items.entry: Beliefs highlighted in folk horror stories might not be true.

items.name: Secret Traditions
items.entry: Community members typically hide their beliefs, perhaps by physically obscuring them or manipulating others in power.

items.name: Strangeness in Specificity
items.entry: Art, symbols, tools, celebrations, and other trappings of belief make a community's traditions more specific and eerie.

entries.name: Folk Horror Villains
entries: Antagonistic forces in folk horror usually are community leaders—paragons of the strange beliefs central to the horror's narrative. Outsiders might have a chance to adopt the community's ways, but otherwise locals consider them heretics or corrupting elements.

entries.name: Villain Identities
entries: Folk horror villains are manipulative, leading others to follow inscrutable traditions. Choose from or roll on the Folk Horror Villains table to inspire villains appropriate to this genre.

entries.caption: Folk Horror Villains
rows: A secluded temple's high priest who needs to find the perfect sacrifice.
An erinyes that appears when youngsters speak a rhyme into a darkened mirror.
A night hag that dwells in the dreams of those who drink a special ergot tea.
A shape-shifter that takes on the appearance of the last person it fed on.
A wicker giant that animates during the new moon, punishing the unwary.
A mayor who behaves in archaic ways so villagers don't enrage an ancient ghost.
A treant who demands corpses to replace trees cleared by a town's construction.
A mysterious robed figure who wears a horned mask and leads a silent cult.
A bloodthirsty forest guardian that resembles a deer with two heads.
A shadow that appears only when individuals look away from the woods' tree line.

entries.name: Villain Torments
entries: Folk horror villains have been consumed by the traditions or land they embody, causing them to fail in their duties or their rites to spin out of control. Choose from or roll on the Folk Horror Torments to determine how these twisted traditions torment the villain.

entries.caption: Folk Horror Torments
rows: Knows the beliefs are false but keeps up the facade to maintain power.
Adheres to an ancient faith and desperately works to convert nonbelievers.
Seeks to transform their body into a vessel or gate for the subject of their belief.
Now appears as a bloodthirsty amalgamation of various forest creatures.
Is haunted by judgmental ancestral spirits.
Can appear only at dawn and dusk.
Has partially melded into a hallowed tree.
Can no longer commune with their deity, driving them to ever bloodier sacrifices.

entries.name: Folk Horror Adventures
entries: In folk horror tales, characters often discover that their beliefs aren't as universally held as they assumed—and provide no defense against those who reject those beliefs.

entries.name: Adventure Hooks
entries: Folk horror plots often involve outsiders to a group discovering unsettling practices or traditions. Choose from or roll on the Folk Horror Plots to inspire your folk horror adventures.

entries.caption: Folk Horror Plots
rows: Anyone entering the city on horseback is imprisoned and sentenced to death.
A cult requests help summoning a demonic force to combat a greater evil.
The townspeople protect a violent hag, whom they call "grandmother."
After an estate party, the residents adopt the characters, refusing to let them leave.
The characters bear an uncanny resemblance to the founders of a secret village.
Townsfolk disappear for days before reappearing with missing memories.
Strangers hand out wax earplugs to protect others from "the choir."
Every night, parents let their children follow floating lights into the forest.
Anyone who stays in the village overnight loses their shadow to the looming woods.

entries.name: Adventure Settings
entries: Folk horror stories often take place in isolated or rural areas, but they could be set anywhere insular communities thrive or traditions stagnate. Choose from or roll on the Folk Horror Settings to inspire locations for your adventures.

entries.caption: Folk Horror Settings
rows: A copse of dense, blackened woods.
A mansion built around a stone circle.
A field where paths grow in corridor-like patterns leading to a ruin at the center.
A telepathic collective that townsfolk join by ingesting a rare fungus.
A hag's hut that stands atop a growing hill of rotten sweets.
A glacier that residents never leave.
A cavern where the glowing bones of an otherworldly being jut from the walls.
A countryside with stretches of hayfields and colorful barns.
Rival villages engaged in an age-old war.
A deserted chapel that has been burned down and rebuilt a thousand times.

entries.name: Folk Horror Monsters
entries: Eerie traditions and unnatural alliances with monsters fill folk horror tales. When certain behaviors have taken hold over generations, even the strangest practices and most dangerous beings might be accepted as part of society. The Folk Horror Monsters table presents monsters particularly appropriate to such adventures. Unless otherwise noted, creatures in this table appear in the {@book Monster Manual|XMM}.

entries.name: Ghost Stories
entries: Ghost stories number among the more psychologically elaborate genres of horror. Only by revealing tales of tragedy and past wrongs can heroes bring peace to forces that share their suffering with the living. Examples include {@i The Woman in Black} and {@i The Haunting of Hill House}.

entries.name: Ghost Story Principles
entries: Consider the following principles when creating Darklords and domains inspired by ghost stories:

items.name: Mirror of Humanity
items.entry: Ghosts are a way to examine the nature of the soul, mortality, and history.

items.name: Naive Heroes
items.entry: Heroes are purehearted or unsuspecting individuals whose resolve is shaken by the story's events. Look for ways to test heroes' psychology with your hauntings.

items.name: Resolving a Haunt
items.entry: Heroes need a way to put spirits to rest. Once you reveal the story, ensure the way to combat the haunting is clear.

items.name: Specificity
items.entry: Even the smallest details can reveal a haunting's story. A simple locket or portrait might contain clues that explain a haunting.

items.name: Ties to the Living
items.entry: Personal ties give ghost stories weight. Consider connecting the heroes to spirits in ways they won't predict, such as making a phantasmal villain a hero's ancestor.

entries.name: Ghost Story Villains
entries: In ghost stories, spirits often represent instruments of supernatural justice, causing those responsible for their deaths to suffer. Spirits also can represent grief and the need for closure.

entries.name: Villain Identities
entries: While the villains in ghost stories are often spirits or haunted places, they also could be the individuals who provoked a haunting to begin with. Choose from or roll on the Ghost Story Villains table to inspire villains appropriate to this genre.

entries.caption: Ghost Story Villains
rows: A medium who gains power over spirits by feeding victims to them.
The spirit of a long-dead murderer who continues to kill in death.
A ghostly ancestor who refuses to relinquish control of their kin.
A priest who marks the unworthy for sacrifice to the cathedral's hungry spirits
A phantom rider who sweeps through the village, stealing away the living.
An unbound spirit that repeatedly manifests in victims' nightmares.
The capricious phantasm of an amoral accident victim who torments the living.
The spirit of a former tyrant who demands sacrifices from their old domain.
A ghost hunter who inflicts hauntings on clients—then charges to remove the spirits.
The spirit of an evil captain who lurks near their wrecked vessel, sabotaging passing ships.

entries.name: Villain Torments
entries: Whether they're living monsters or ghosts, villains in ghost stories are the architects of their own tragedies. Choose from or roll on the Ghost Story Torments table to determine how the villain's past painfully manifests.

entries.caption: Ghost Story Torments
rows: Can't touch anyone without stealing a precious memory.
Can temporarily release spirits from the villain's elaborate scars.
Is hounded by spirits that each represent an emotion the villain can no longer feel.
Drains the life from any animal they see.
Has lost the ability to feel physical sensation.
Can be seen only in reflective surfaces.
Is bound within a building; leaving would permanently destroy them.
Can communicate only through music and writing.
Siphons the color from objects they possess.

entries.name: Ghost Story Adventures
entries: Ghost story adventures deal with learning the story behind a haunting and ultimately resolving it. They benefit from preserving the mystery behind a spirit's motivations until the heroes discover the truth.

entries.name: Adventure Hooks
entries: Though the structure—learn about a haunting and resolve it—might seem straightforward, ghost story plots can explore philosophical issues like the burden of ancestry and the nature of the soul. Choose from or roll on the Ghost Story Plots table to inspire your ghost stories.

entries.caption: Ghost Story Plots
rows: Murder victims linger as ghosts until someone solves their murders.
Bloody graffiti keeps appearing on the city's walls.
A stubborn miser must visit their haunted family home to put their family to rest.
A mob of angry spirits besieges the local temple on the winter solstice each year.
The adventurers must solve the murder of a ghost who can say only three words.
A ship—with the same name as a vessel lost at sea decades prior—arrives at port.
Rumors abound of a ritual allowing a living individual to trade places with the dead.
The ghosts of an actor's rivals haunt the local theater.
A noble accidentally broke a family urn, unleashing their cruel ancestral spirits.
Every full moon, spirits in a graveyard rise to seek vengeance for a mass crime.

entries.name: Adventure Settings
entries: Ghost stories are intensely personal, and adventures within this genre take place in a setting dripping with tragic history. Choose from or roll on the Ghost Story Settings table to inspire locations for your adventures.

entries.caption: Ghost Story Settings
rows: A decrepit mansion where, for years, nobles killed the staff to keep their secrets.
A graveyard city-state where all living residents are grave keepers.
An opulent yet abandoned library, filled with haunted artifacts.
The site of an ancient battle that permanently devastated the land.
A nation where the residents observe grueling rituals to appease the angry dead.
An expansive forest in which a cruel noble once hunted the poor for sport.
A ruined barn where dozens of remarkable animals lost their lives in a fire.
A picturesque cliff where visiting couples often mysteriously fall to their deaths.
An attic in which a hateful spirit has been sealed for decades.
A dilapidated lighthouse overlooking a black-sand beach.

entries.name: Ghost Story Monsters
entries: Creatures that embody or serve as a response to past tragedies often feature in ghost stories. Don't limit yourself to incorporeal Undead when creating your own hauntings. The Ghost Story Monsters table presents monsters particularly appropriate to such adventures. Unless otherwise noted, creatures in this table appear in the {@book Monster Manual|XMM}.

entries.name: Gothic Horror
entries: Gothic horror deals with the terror within. It shatters the illusion of humanity by holding a mirror up to us and forcing us to examine what we truly are and what we pretend to be. In gothic horror, we—not some distant entity—are the architects of our destruction. Examples include {@i Dracula} and {@i Frankenstein}.

entries.name: Gothic Horror Principles
entries: Consider the following principles when creating Darklords and domains with gothic horror themes:

items.name: Brave Heroes
items.entry: Gothic heroes are often virtuous, deeply passionate, or courageous. Adventures should test characters' beliefs and morality.

items.name: Foreboding
items.entry: Atmosphere and a sense of dread are key. Locations are often decrepit or foreboding.

items.name: High Emotions
items.entry: Gothic stories include intense, exaggerated emotions. Romances, rivalries, and life-changing events are common.

items.name: Sacrifice
items.entry: Sacrifices feature prominently in gothic stories. Give characters heart-wrenching choices.

items.name: Unassuming, Unrepentant Villains
items.entry: Gothic villains are unrepentantly evil, but this shouldn't be immediately obvious. Drop hints about your villain's awful secrets before revealing them fully.

entries.name: Gothic Horror Villains
entries: Though thoroughly wicked, villains in gothic horror tales act reserved or unassuming until they reveal their true nature. Any intriguing figure with a dark secret can serve as a gothic horror villain.

entries.name: Villain Identities
entries: Gothic villains are most effective when they are slowly revealed as shockingly cruel, immoral, or the antithesis of good. Choose from or roll on the Gothic Horror Villains table to inspire villains appropriate to this genre.

entries.caption: Gothic Horror Villains
rows: A reclusive noble who uses their vampiric reputation to terrorize their vassals.
A vain socialite who made a bargain with a fell power to retain their youth.
A scientist obsessed with creating the perfect poison, machine, or life-form.
A beloved magnate who abducts commoners to use in beauty rituals.
A celebrity who murders openly but uses their charm to avoid repercussions.
Someone who loves a monstrous creature and does anything to keep it safe.
A wealthy heir who manipulates the ambitious into committing terrible deeds.
A poisoner who seeks to manipulate history through targeted killings.
An artist who concocts terrible accidents to inspire their art.
A monster hunter who accuses those they consider sinful of being monsters.

entries.name: Villain Torments
entries: Self-debasement and self-loathing lurk at the heart of gothic villains' evil, whether they indulge in vices or are consumed with misanthropy. Choose from or roll on the Gothic Horror Torments table to determine how temptation and dramatic emotions torment the villain.

entries.caption: Gothic Horror Torments
rows: Inherited unlimited wealth, but all finery they wear turns into ash.
Is covered in tattoos, which magically update to illustrate all their sins.
Turns into a lead statue when the sun rises.
Is incomparably beautiful, but locals perceive them as a beast.
Sees evidence of their impending doom everywhere.
Extinguishes any light shone on them.
Craves company but is cursed to forever wear a mask and obscure their identity.

entries.name: Gothic Horror Adventures
entries: The themes of gothic horror adventures center around shattering the lies we trick ourselves into believing. Ominous history, poetic tragedy, and supernatural forces are staples of gothic horror.

entries.name: Adventure Hooks
entries: The dichotomy between goodness and evil, reckonings for wicked deeds, and dire choices are strong themes in gothic horror plots. Choose from or roll on the Gothic Horror Plots table to inspire your gothic horror adventures.

entries.caption: Gothic Horror Plots
rows: The city's rulers secretly are warring hags, wererats, and vampires.
A scientist, never seen and known only through their writing, has disappeared.
A repentant immortal requests help in losing centuries of painful memories.
A beloved social figure turns into a murderous living doll every night.
A pair of spirits brings unspeakable tragedy to any romantic couple in town.
A generational curse forces villagers to sacrifice themselves.
Every night, one random guest of an opulent inn chokes on their own blood.
A serial killer impales victims on a certain monument in a cruel mockery of art.
Terrible noises sound from a dilapidated mansion; those who visit are never seen again.
As the result of abhorrent experiments, blood-drained creatures roam the streets.

entries.name: Adventure Settings
entries: Gothic horror settings have an underlying sinister air, and encounters often take place in forlorn structures where depravity runs rampant. Choose from or roll on the Gothic Horror Settings table to inspire locations for your adventures.

entries.caption: Gothic Horror Settings
rows: A lavish garden that siphons life from visitors to maintain its beauty.
A ruined cathedral devoted to transforming a prophesied being.
A laboratory where preserved body parts carry the minds of their former owners.
A forest of eternal night where bloodthirsty creatures live in fragile peace.
A rectory where the stained glass windows hold the trapped souls of the pious.
A city where all who die are cast in plaster and used to adorn the streets.
An island where the inhabitants make sacrifices to avoid eerie transformations.
An artist's paradise where cruelties are elevated to terrible and beautiful art forms.
A castle in which visitors are transformed into objects or animals.
A ruined fortress haunted by a monstrous yet docile creature.

entries.name: Gothic Horror Monsters
entries: Creatures imbued with tragedy or abominable origins work well in gothic horror adventures. The Gothic Horror Monsters table presents monsters particularly appropriate to such adventures. Unless otherwise noted, creatures in this table appear in the {@book Monster Manual|XMM}.

entries.caption: Gothic Horror Monsters
rows: {@creature Inquisitor of the Mind Fire|RHW},* {@creature Inquisitor of the Sword|RHW},* {@creature Inquisitor of the Tome|RHW},* {@creature Vampire Nosferatu|RHW}*

entries.name: Disaster Horror
entries: In disaster horror, the world has fallen to ruin or it's getting there fast. The unthinkable has happened, and society is collapsing. In these tales, you might replace a monster or villain with cascading catastrophes like those brought about by a meteorite strike or volcanic eruption. Regardless of a hero's power, the best-timed strike or the most powerful spell might not fix a city ruined by an earthquake or a world ravaged by plagues.

entries.name: Disaster Horror Features
entries: Consider the following principles when creating adventures with disaster horror themes:

items.name: Nature of Disaster
items.entry: The disasters in your horror adventures need not be natural. Magical disruptions, temporal shifts, and violent interplanar rifts might sow supernatural chaos. Wars and relentless monster hordes—including legions of zombies or demons—also can cause domain-changing ruin.

items.name: Society's Downfall
items.entry: In disaster horror, society breaks down. Inns are closed, healers are few or overtaxed, and anarchy rises as governments crumble.

items.name: What It Takes to Survive
items.entry: Scared innocents, opportunistic scavengers, and leaderless institutions (military or otherwise) seek to survive. The hopeless acts of others might prove more horrifying than the disaster.

entries.name: Occult Detective Stories
entries: Occult detective stories straddle the line between fantasy, mystery, and horror. For the occult detective, solving paranormal mysteries is part of the job. In these adventures, supernatural criminals or monsters pursue nefarious ends while the characters act as detectives: interpreting events, seeking patterns, deducing goals, and ultimately putting an end to the threat. Investigation and cleverness take center stage in these adventures, although—as in the noir stories that inspire the genre—there's plenty of room for action.

entries.name: Occult Detective Story Features
entries: Consider the following principles when creating adventures with occult detective themes:

items.name: Lighter Frights
items.entry: Occult detective adventures can be just as terrifying as other horror adventures but might be lighter in tone. Adventurers might encounter the results of horrific deeds rather than experiencing them firsthand and might face a foe only once they're prepared.

items.name: Supernatural Twists
items.entry: Consider how monsters, magic, or other supernatural forces might lend a thrilling twist to criminal enterprises. How will the characters catch an invisible assassin or a thief who pilfers extradimensional spaces?

items.name: Threading Hints
items.entry: Once you know the villain's mode of operation, consider how the villain might give themself away. Even if you're not sure, listen to the characters' plans and give them opportunities to feel brilliant in the course of their investigations.

entries.name: Psychological Horror
entries: Psychological horror stories create suspense by heightening or calling into question characters' states of mind, emotions, and perceptions. These tales often highlight the difference between what characters think and how they behave.

entries.name: Psychological Horror Features
entries: Consider the following principles when creating adventures with psychological horror themes:

items.name: Fears as Metaphors
items.entry: Common anxieties and fears can become metaphors for antagonists in psychological horror. Fear of judgment by one's peers might manifest as a jury of nothics, while fear of change could be represented by a medusa that petrifies those who threaten the status quo.

items.name: Mental Illness Isn't Horror
items.entry: Some classic psychological horror tales stigmatize mental illness. Avoid this trope in your adventures.

items.name: Unreliable Information
items.entry: Uncertainty and paranoia shape psychological horror stories. Characters might experience a deadly encounter only to wake up at the end, not having realized they were asleep. Before undermining characters' senses, identify what's driving these skewed perceptions and how characters might overcome them.

entries.name: Slasher Horror
entries: Slasher horror contends with relentless killers, which might be people or monsters. These adventures revel in grisly details, the suspense of an impending showdown with a powerful foe, and the fear that death waits right around the corner.

entries.name: Slasher Horror Features
entries: Consider the following principles when creating adventures with slasher horror themes:

items.name: Solo Antagonist
items.entry: Slasher horror stories typically include one major antagonist that threatens a group. This might be a particularly large or cunning beast, a murderer who terrorizes a neighborhood, a monster stalking a town, or a supernatural menace that spreads a signature cause of death.

items.name: Targets
items.entry: Consider who the villain's targets are and why the villain is preying on that group. Is it out of hunger or for revenge, or does the creature have a deep-seated need to kill?

items.name: Violence and Infamy
items.entry: Antagonists in slasher horror are often infamous, akin to urban legends, with numerous victims. Consider how the killer avoids capture and what makes the killer's actions warrant their infamy.

data.name: Chapter 5: Denizens of the Mists

entries.title: A grim dullahan strikes down its victim, claiming another grisly death's head as a follower

entries: Terrors haunt the Mists. This chapter begins with advice to help you, the DM, use monsters to create frightful encounters. Following this is a collection of horrifying monsters and stalwart monster hunters for your adventures in the Domains of Dread.

entries.name: Horror Monsters
entries: Cultivating mystery and menace around the monstrous foes in your adventures can be a simple matter. Here are techniques you can use to transform any monster into a horror to haunt characters' nightmares.

entries.name: Monstrous Origins
entries: Unique monsters are singularly terrifying. Consider presenting the monster central to your adventures as a one-of-a-kind terror. Such a creature might be a fluke of nature, the product of insidious magic, a purposefully terrifying creation of the Dark Powers, or a being with an even more unsettling background. Use the tips for creating Darklords and drawing inspiration from different genres of horror in {@area chapter 4|34d|x} to create unique origins for the most important threats in your adventures.
Different origin stories suggest diverse paths for adventurers to follow when investigating these creatures, learning their weaknesses, and ensuring nothing like them ever menaces again.

entries.name: Terrifying Language
entries: Describe monsters in vivid ways. A terror chasing characters could just "follow" them, but instead it might "wriggle," "surge," or "galumph" after them. Descriptive language underscores how a creature behaves and conjures a visceral description of the monster. Unexpected descriptions can emphasize a creature's bizarre nature and unsettle the players. For example, it's one thing to say a snake "slithers," but another to say a sinister shadow or cruel witch does.

Use unsettling descriptions and similes. For example, a vine blight's constricting vines might resemble "noose-like cords" or "oily digits." Try less literal descriptions—even nonsensical ones. For example, a flesh golem might howl like "an earthquake in a mortuary" or "a charnel pit that's learned to sob."

entries.name: Notorious Monsters
entries: Treat creatures central to your horror adventures as infamous threats. The more you foreshadow their menace or reveal that they have threatened others before, the larger such creatures loom. Build dread by giving monsters reputations that suggest the monsters' form, deeds, or peculiarities while letting players' imaginations embellish details.

For example, tales might describe a skeletal horror that whistles cheerfully while committing brutal acts. Characters might hear rumors of the Whistling Fiend's melodies becoming haunting earworms for survivors of the fiend's attacks. A party seeking the monster might hear it whistling in the Mists long before they confront it. All that time, characters can speculate about their enemy's nature, but ultimately the Whistling Fiend might be any threat you choose.

entries.name: Mysterious Monsters
entries: Use conflicting descriptions and strange behaviors to cultivate mystery around your monster. The less characters know about a creature, the more they'll speculate about terrifying possibilities.

You can develop uncertainty about a creature even after it appears. Keep players guessing about an adventure's threat by referring to a monster by a flavorful description rather than the name of its stat block. A "zombie" doesn't sound like much of a threat, but a "stumbling mass of fetid skin and shattered bones" might be something else entirely.

entries.name: Weird Traits
entries: By adding unexpected traits to monster stat blocks, you can undermine players' expectations of what a monster is and what it can do. Consider how you can use the {@book Creature Traits|XDMG|2|Creature Traits} list in the "{@book Creating a Creature|XDMG|2|Creating a Creature}" section of the {@book Dungeon Master's Guide|XDMG} to give familiar monsters horrifying twists.

entries.name: Putting It All Together
entries: Combine the techniques in this section to create a unique terror. If you have ideas about what you want your monster to do, write them down. Then develop stories that connect those pieces or reinforce the type of horror you want to create.

entries.title: A zombie beholder's terror is transformed when the monster is recast as a flesh-stitched horror

entries: For example, perhaps you've got an idea for an arrogant mage who sought to prove he didn't need his adventuring companions' protection by constructing a suit of living armor. Considering the bizarre, magical whims of such a character, you choose a {@creature Beholder Zombie|XMM|Zombie Beholder} as the foundation for the mage's animate armor. To reinforce its nature as a magical creation, you change the monster's creature type from Undead to Construct and describe it as being made of stitched-together animal parts. Then you give it the Berserk and Magic Resistance traits of a {@creature Flesh Golem|XMM}. The characters' surprise at unexpectedly facing a beholder might be startling enough, but you add to your story that the mage has become trapped within his living armor as it hunts his former companions. Only by defeating the horror might the characters free what remains of the mage within, represented by the Emissary of Juiblex creature trait from the "{@book Creating a Creature|XDMG|2|Creating a Creature}" section of the {@book Dungeon Master's Guide|XDMG}.

entries.name: Bestiary
entries: The following pages detail monstrous and heroic inhabitants of the Domains of Dread, presented in alphabetical order. The Monsters by Challenge Rating table organizes these stat blocks by Challenge Rating.

entries.title: A new bodytaker podling emerges from a bodytaker plant
Three types of death's heads guard an accursed death's head tree
{@creature Dullahan|RHW}
{@creature Gallows Speaker|RHW}
{@creature Loup Garou|RHW}
A Soul Tattoo on the Flesh of a Priest of Osybus
Clockwise from Front: Relentless Nightmare, Relentless Slasher, and Relentless Juggernaut
Front to Back: Lesser Star Spawn Emissary and Greater Star Spawn Emissary
Left to Right: Inquisitor of the Tome, Inquisitor of the Sword, and Inquisitor of the Mind Fire

data.name: Appendix: Tarokka Deck
entries: Tarokka fortune-telling cards originated among Vistani, who created these cards to guide their travels. Since Vistani entered the Mists, their cards have spread and gained renown as tools for divining the future and charting paths between the Domains of Dread. Now, diviners across many lands seek glimpses of what's to come and answers to unknowable questions from the tarokka. Such diviners have a saying: "No secret is safe from the tarokka."

The tarokka deck is a prop for creating mood-rich fortune-telling scenes in your adventures. It features prominently in the adventure {@adventure Curse of Strahd|CoS}, but you can use it to influence adventures of your own design as well.

entries.name: Composition
entries: A tarokka deck has fifty-four cards, each of which has its own name. Forty of them compose the common deck, which is divided into four suits: coins, glyphs, stars, and swords.

Each suit contains cards numbered one through nine, plus a tenth card called the master of that suit. One can refer to a card by its suit designation or by its name. For instance, the six of stars is also known as the evoker, and the master of coins is the rogue.
The remaining fourteen cards make up the high deck and symbolize powerful and enigmatic forces.
All cards in the tarokka deck and what they represent are detailed at the end of {@area this appendix|3dc|x}.

entries.name: High Deck
entries: The cards that make up the high deck aren't considered one suit, although they all have a crown icon to mark their importance. Each card represents an important power. If one of these cards appears during a reading and contradicts the prediction of an earlier card, Vistani always assume this card takes precedence.

entries.name: Common Deck
entries: The four suits of the common deck are described below.

entries.name: Coins
entries: The suit of coins symbolizes desire for personal and material gain. It also represents gluttony, lust, and obsession. On the side of good, this suit can suggest the accumulation of wealth to benefit a charity or a just cause. On the side of evil, it embodies the worst aspects of greed.

entries.name: Glyphs
entries: The suit of glyphs symbolizes faith, spirituality, and inner strength. It is the suit of priests and those who devote themselves to the service of a deity, a higher power, or a heightened philosophy. It symbolizes health and healing, as well as illness and disease. On the side of good, this suit represents willpower and dedication. On the side of evil, it signifies weakness of character, self-doubt, and betrayal of one's ideals or beliefs.

entries.name: Stars
entries: The suit of stars symbolizes the desire for personal power and control over things beyond mortal ken. It is the suit of mages, sages, and intellectuals. It also represents the triumph of magic, science, and reason over religion, mysticism, and superstition.

entries.name: Swords
entries: The suit of swords symbolizes aggression and violence. It is the suit of warriors, be they paladins, soldiers, or mercenaries. It also symbolizes the power of governments and leaders, whether noble or corrupt.

entries.name: Prop Tarokka Decks
entries: Tarokka decks for use in your game are available separately, with each card featuring imagery to aid you in creating portentous fortunes. If you don't have a tarokka deck, you can use a regular deck of playing cards instead. To do so, separate the numbered cards from the face cards and jokers, and treat them as two separate decks: the common deck (the numbered cards) and the high deck (the face cards and jokers).

entries.name: Common Deck
entries: Each suit in a regular deck of playing cards corresponds to a suit in the tarokka deck.

items: Hearts = Glyphs
Spades = Swords
Diamonds = Coins
Clubs = Stars

entries: An ace represents a one in the tarokka deck, while a ten in a regular deck represents a master card in the tarokka deck.

entries.name: High Deck
entries: Each jack, queen, king, and joker corresponds to a card in the tarokka high deck.

entries.name: Tarokka Readings
entries: Tarokka decks aid fortune tellers in revealing information about specific individuals. The focus of a tarokka reading might be the individual having their fortune told or someone else. A fortune teller randomly selects or invites another to choose what tarokka card best represents the focus of the reading. To begin the tarokka reading, this card is laid face up in position 1 in the pattern below.
The fortune teller then draws four additional cards, laying one face down in each of the other numbered positions in the pattern. These positions each represent something about the focus:

items.name: Position 1
items.entry: The focus of the reading and the lens through which the other cards are interpreted

items.name: Position 2
items.entry: The past, something that might or might not be apparent to those other than the focus

items.name: Position 3
items.entry: What opposes the focus, whether literal opponents or obstructing fortunes

items.name: Position 4
items.entry: The future, an omen of what's to come and a fate that might still be changed

items.name: Position 5
items.entry: What aids the focus, whether literal allies or advantageous events

entries: The reading proceeds with the fortune teller revealing the significance of each card's position and what the card represents, as guided by the descriptions later in {@area this appendix|3dc|x}. After a card is interpreted, the card in the next position is revealed. This continues until all five cards have been interpreted. The fortune teller might reveal more details (see the following section) or claim that the cards remain silent on certain points. The fortune teller and characters then might speculate about the reading's broader meanings. Once you're satisfied with what's revealed, the reading ends.

entries.name: Tarokka Techniques
entries: The tarokka deck is a prop for inspiring adventures and creating dramatic fortune-telling scenes. Through a mix of creative interpretation and card manipulation, a DM can influence what the tarokka reveals and the accuracy of its revelations. Consider the following techniques during tarokka readings.

entries.name: Stacking the Deck
entries: You can use sleight of hand tricks to ensure tarokka cards appear where you desire during a reading. Select the desired cards before the tarokka reading and put them in the order you want to draw them so you can produce them effortlessly and place them in the desired positions. Cards might be stacked on the top of the deck or drawn from the bottom. Stacking the deck is useful for ensuring specific results or creating patterns that appear repeatedly between readings. Avoid letting players notice you're stacking the deck, as it undermines the mystery of that and future tarokka readings.

entries.name: Dramatic Interpretations

entries.title: Orphir Brindletop, a member of the Keepers of the Feather, consults the tarokka

entries: When interpreting tarokka cards, you can elaborate on the card descriptions in {@area this appendix|3dc|x}, reframing those details and adjusting them to better suit your adventure. Use these techniques to add onto and adjust the meanings of tarokka cards:

items.name: Broad Interpretation
items.entry: Highlight broader card details, like the meanings of suits and the generalities they encompass, over card specifics.

items.name: Card Imagery
items.entry: Focus on specifics in a card's art, like the numbers of objects or the positions of figures, to suggest a deeper meaning.

items.name: Contextual Meanings
items.entry: Suggest that multiple cards of the same suit or in specific relation to one another have greater or lesser meaning.

items.name: Diviner Experience
items.entry: Reveal that a card arrangement has a specific meaning, or that the fortune teller has seen it in an ominous instance before.

items.name: Foreshadowing
items.entry: Highlight details that align with impending events of which only you are aware.

items.name: Leading Questions
items.entry: Have a fortune teller ask questions that lead characters to connect the reading with past events or their existing knowledge.

entries.name: Manipulating Fate
entries: You control fate in your adventures. If you don't know what a tarokka card means during a reading, note that and fulfill its prophecy in the future. For example, if the four of glyphs appears in a reading, its imagery might appear later as a shepherd NPC, a hooked staff, a wandering sheep, four runes, or the like. You can then tell the players about the connection between the image and the reading.
Additionally, listen in on players' speculations about the meaning of tarokka readings, then use those suggestions to influence future revelations in your adventures.

entries.caption: High Deck
rows: The importance of some physical object that must be obtained, protected, or destroyed at all costs
Great rage or passion; something bestial or malevolent hiding in plain sight or lurking just below the surface
Defeat, failure, and despair; the loss of something or someone important, without which one feels incomplete
A powerful evil individual; one whose goals have enormous and far-reaching consequences
Isolation and imprisonment; a prisoner of their own beliefs
The looming past; the return of an old enemy or the discovery of a secret buried long ago
The imminent death of one rightly or wrongly convicted of a crime; false accusations and unjust prosecution
Death; disastrous loss of wealth or property, a horrible defeat, or the end of a bloodline
A being of great importance whose life is in danger (who might be helpless or simply unaware of the peril)
The presence of a spy or a minion of some greater power; an encounter with a puppet or an underling
Something unexpected or mysterious that can't be avoided; a great quest or journey that will try one's spirit
A hidden source of information; a fortunate turn of events; a secret potential for good
Inspiration and keen intellect; a future event, the outcome of which will hinge on a clever mind
One who has been compromised or led astray by temptation or foolishness; one who tempts others for evil ends

entries.caption: Swords
rows: Strength and force personified; violence; those who use force to accomplish their goals
Justice and revenge for great wrongs; those on a quest to rid the world of great evil
Just and noble warriors; those who live by a code of honor and integrity
War and sacrifice; the stamina to endure great hardship
Inner strength and fortitude; those who fight for power or wealth
Great heroes; a sudden reversal of fate; the triumph of the underdog over a mighty enemy
The brutal and barbaric side of warfare; bloodlust; those with a bestial nature
Bigotry, intolerance, and xenophobia; a mysterious presence or newcomer
All that is wrong with leadership; those who rule through fear and violence
The coming of suffering or merciless cruelty; one who is irredeemably evil or sadistic

entries.caption: Stars
rows: Mystery and riddles; the unknown; those who crave magical power and great knowledge
A new discovery; the coming of unexpected things; unforeseen consequences and chaos
The pursuit of knowledge tempered by wisdom; truth and honesty; sages and prophecy
Inner turmoil that comes from confusion, fear of failure, or false information
Those guided by logic and reasoning; warnings of an overlooked clue or piece of information
The triumph of nature over civilization; natural disasters and bountiful harvests
Magical or supernatural power that can't be controlled; magic for destructive ends
Lies and deceit; grand conspiracies; secret societies; the presence of a dupe or a saboteur
Unnatural events and unhealthy obsessions; those who follow a destructive path
The coming of an unexpected supernatural threat; those who think of themselves as gods

entries.caption: Coins
rows: Anyone for whom money is important; those who believe money is the key to their success
Those who like money yet give it up freely; likable rogues and rapscallions
Charity and giving on a grand scale; those who use wealth to fight evil and sickness
Commerce; smuggling and black markets; fair and equitable trades
A rare commodity or business opportunity; deceitful or dangerous business transactions
Like-minded individuals joined together in a common goal; pride in one's work
Sudden change in economic status or fortune
Those who steal or burgle; loss of property, beauty, innocence, friendship, or reputation
Corruption; honesty in an otherwise corrupt government or organization
Hoarded wealth; those who are irreversibly unhappy or think money is meaningless

entries.caption: Glyphs
rows: Enlightenment; those who follow a deity, a system of values, or a higher purpose
Serenity; inner strength and self-reliance; supreme confidence bereft of arrogance
Those who spread wisdom to others; warnings of the spread of fear and ignorance
Healing; a contagious illness, disease, or curse; those who practice the healing arts
Those who protect others; one who bears a burden far too great to be shouldered alone
The ambivalence and cruelty of nature and those who feel drawn to it; inner turmoil
A fundamental change brought on by one whose beliefs are being put to the test
Liars; those who profess to believe one thing but actually believe another
Strict adherence to a code or a belief; those who plot, plan, and scheme
Betrayal by someone close and trusted; a weakening or loss of faith
